codewordLabels.HAUNT-SEEN-BY-CHILD: the child has let you be seen

blocks.s: You wake on a chaise the colour of old wine, in a parlour cold enough to show your breath, and you do not remember lying down. You do not remember anything before this room. Not your name. Not the year. Not how a person ends up asleep in a house this cold, with frost feathering the inside of the windows and a dead candle on the side table beside a single card.

Far off and above, someone is humming. Three notes, rising. Your right hand has drifted down to your side, palm open, fingers half-curled, holding the shape of a smaller hand that should be in it. There is nothing there. You don't know why you did that, and you fold the hand shut, and the not-knowing is worse than the cold.

choices.label: Read the card
Rise and look at the room

blocks.s: Your thumb works the catch and the locket opens and inside is nothing. Not an empty frame. Not a faded face. A clean small oval of nothing, as if a portrait was lifted out, or scratched out, the way the faces upstairs were scratched out, by someone who could not bear the picture and could not throw the locket in the river.

You close it. The hollow of it weighs more than any face could. You are walking through a house made by a person methodically unmaking every likeness of what they lost, and failing, every time, at the last small step. And the cold that goes through you in the cellar is not the cellar's, because you think you might be the one who held the knife.

choices.label: Climb toward the landing

3.title: The Stair That Isn't There (an ending)

blocks.s: You step out onto the nothing, because the voice swore, because being known felt like being safe, because the voice was warm and certain and the one you most wanted to obey.

There is no stair. There was never a stair. The voice told you so itself, once, and then offered it anyway, and you took the offer because you had decided the warning was the lie. You drop into the dark below the third step, and the last thing you hear, dwindling above you, is the voice, not laughing, not gloating, only sorry, finishing your final thought a half-beat early as it always has. "should not have trusted the cue you wanted most," it says, gently, to no one, already turning to the next thought, the next newcomer, the next warm certain lie it cannot stop itself telling.

The cold finds a floor, and the floor is bare earth. Down here the house stops pretending to be a house. It is a cellar the size of a churchyard, and along one wall a man sits with his back to the stone, enormous, still, cracking one knuckle and counting under his breath. Four. Five. Six. A man counting to keep from doing something.

He doesn't look up. "Far enough," he says, not unkindly. "I'm rationing. There's a thing this house wants me to be, and every step toward the middle makes it easier to be it. So I sit at the edge and I count." Past him the dark thickens around two shapes: a low mound of turned earth, and a stair going up.

choices.label: Go to the small grave
Sit a while with the big man
Follow the slow clapping deeper into the dark
Find the low passage that goes further down
Climb toward the landing

blocks.s: The stairs let you out onto a landing lined with windows, and beyond the glass is not the night you expected but a grey nowhere, a fog with shapes moving in it that might be a town. In the nearest pane, where your own reflection ought to be, there is a child instead, small, holding the string of a balloon that drifts against the glass from the wrong side, as though the window were underwater.
The child looks at you. Your hand opens at your side again, on its own, reaching up for a height that is not there.

choices.label: Let the child see that you see them
Try the window-latch and climb out toward the fog
Look away and go on

blocks.s: You climb out onto the sill and you let go into the soft grey, toward the patient road, and the house does not stop you, because the house never stops anyone leaving. That is the whole cruelty of it.

You fall, and the fog has no bottom, only more fog, and the road you were so sure you saw was the house showing you the exit it most wanted you to take. The cheap one. The early one. The one that costs you the part that mattered before you ever found out what it was. You don't land. You thin. Far above, a window screams shut on its hinge, and somewhere a pencil scratches you off a list, and the grey closes over the place where you were as if you had been imagined, and then, mercifully, forgotten.
You turn the card at the pulled-out chair. No name on it. Only the blind seal again, and when your fingers find it the card is warm, warmer than the cold room, warm the way the playbill was warm.

The chair is yours. The house laid a place for you and pulled out your seat and kept it warm against your coming, and it did all of that before you opened your eyes, which means it knew you were on your way. You are not a guest who wandered in out of the weather. You were expected. You always were.

choices.label: Set the card down and go find the others

blocks.s: The sourceless light dims, and the landing turns, for a moment, into a set. A summer afternoon staged across it, whole and warm and false, light coming through leaves that are not there, and in it two figures, a tall one and a small one, hand in hand, walking away from you toward a door. You can't see their faces. You don't need to. The set of the small one's shoulders, the tall one shortening its stride to match, the joined hands swinging, all of it drops into you and turns.

At the door the small figure turns back, the way children do, to check the tall one is still following. And the memory stops there, on the turn, on the looking-back, held on the one frame you would never have chosen to keep forever. Your hand is open at your side. The house watches to see which way your face breaks.

choices.label: Refuse it, as the girl taught you, and walk through it to the center
Reach for the small turning figure

blocks.s: You disobey him before he has finished the warning, because you came in carrying something and you will not set it down. You cup it in your mind the way you would cup a coal off the fire: a small warmth, a weight in the curl of your fingers, the ghost of a smaller hand that fit there once.
You can't see the memory. You can only feel its temperature. The Caretaker watches you do it and says nothing, which is worse than if he had argued.

choices.label: Go deeper into the house

blocks.s: Something is climbing your throat. A shape, a name, a fact about what you lost, and it wants out the way held breath wants out, and you think that if you only said it, only named it aloud to this kind tired man, you would not have to be so alone with it.

The Caretaker sees it coming. His hand goes to the lamp and the latch clicks, fast, an alarm. "Don't. Whatever you're about to tell me, the house is closer to you than I am, and it's a better listener. Say a thing you love out loud in here and you've set its place at the table. Swallow it. Swallow it and walk."

choices.label: Say it anyway, because you cannot carry it alone
Swallow it and walk

blocks.s: You find the stairs by feel and by patience, counting each one under your own foot before you trust it, refusing every shortcut. The third stair from the top is indeed not there, a clean drop into a dark you don't investigate, and you step over the gap, because you counted, and you knew.

The voice in the vent goes quiet, and when it speaks again it is far behind you and it does not sound angry. "learning," it murmurs, finishing a thought you didn't know you were having. "Good. Never trust the cue you want most. Mine especially." You go on down toward the landing, where the house has been arranging everything to meet you.

choices.label: Go down to the landing

blocks.s: "Why I stay." Eight. "Because I've seen what's in the middle for me. House looked at me and didn't see a man. Saw a use. Something to put between the grieving one and the door. A wall with hands." Nine, slow. "Worst of it, the part fits. I'm big, I'm angry, and there've been times in my life I wanted to put my hands through a problem instead of round it. House didn't invent that. Just found it and built a room for it."

He looks at you then, eyes wet and steady. "So I count. Every number's me deciding, again, not to be the thing they cast. You'll get cast too. Whatever your part is, it'll fit. That's how you'll know to take it off."

choices.label: Sit a while longer
Climb to the landing

blocks.s: The kitchen is colder than the parlour and a good deal older than the house, a flagstone room with the hearth gone to grey ash. Among the dust on the long table sits one clean thing: an open tin of salt, the lid already off, set out as if for you.
You know two things without being told them. Salt is a line the House respects. And the House keeps an inventory of what you take.

choices.label: Pocket the salt
Search the cold hearth first
Leave it and find the others

blocks.s: You take a dust sheet by the corner and draw it off, and the moving air lifts the dust in a slow grey breath. Under it is a portrait in a heavy frame, a family arranged for a painter, stiff and proud and holding still. Every face has been scratched out, gouged down to the weave of the canvas. Every face but one: the smallest figure, low and to the side, a child caught mid-turn as if about to bolt out of the frame.
The scratching is fresh. The little curls of torn canvas still catch the light. Somebody did this lately, and did it carefully, and could not, at the end, make themselves do the last small face.

choices.label: Look closer at the child the scratching spared
Put the sheet back and step away

blocks.s: The house narrows down to a single door, and the door is ajar, and through the gap is a warmth that has no business in all this cold and a light the colour of a memory kept too carefully. The room beyond is the reason the House was built. You know that standing here. You know it has been waiting for you in particular, and that part of you already knows what is inside it.

lines: THE CURATOR, from everywhere and courteously:
You may look, and learn why you came, and what it will cost to leave.
Or you may turn back, and keep your distance, and your ignorance, a while longer.
The House is patient. The House has all the time you do not.

choices.label: Look into the room
Open your hand and give the house the memory you kept
Step in beside the child who has been letting you be seen
Heed the voice's last cue at the door
Turn and run for the front door
Stay warded at the threshold and do not look tonight

blocks.s: You meet the child's eyes through the glass, and the child's mouth moves, and across a window and a world of fog you hear it as if it were spoken into your ear. "Is it real?"

You don't know which it the child means. You suspect the child doesn't either, and that the question is the only one that counts in this house. The balloon taps the pane, once, twice. Then the glass is only glass, grey and empty, and you are looking at your own breath fogging it, and the cold of the landing comes back all at once.

choices.label: Go on down the upper hall

blocks.s: You sit. He counts. After a while he says, "You've got the look. The ones who came here on purpose have it. The accidents cry. The ones who chose it just go quiet and walk toward the middle like it's owed to them." He cracks the knuckle again. Seven. "Not telling you not to. Telling you the middle costs what you came to keep. Everybody's does."
When you stand he nods once, the nod of a man staying behind on purpose. "Up. The landing's where it all meets. Always meets."

choices.label: Ask him why he stays at the edge
Climb to the landing

blocks.s: The mound is small. Too small. It is the size that stops the breath of anyone who has ever stood at that particular short length of turned earth and understood it. Set into the soil at the head of it is a locket on a tarnished chain, half-buried, as if it was placed there and then half taken back.

You work it free. It weighs almost nothing and it is the heaviest thing you have ever held. Your thumb finds the catch without being shown, and your other hand has gone open and low at your side again, reaching down this time, for a hand the height of a child who would have stood about here.

choices.label: Try the catch and open the locket
Climb toward the landing

blocks.s: You lean in. The spared child is mid-turn, one hand lifted, reaching up and back toward a tall scratched-out shape that would have been standing just behind. A parent's hand the knife took, or the painter never finished. The child's fingers are curled around a blank the painter left, an absence with a shape to it.

Your own hand has come up to match it without asking you. You make yourself put it down. You don't look at the portrait again, because some animal part of you has worked out that the house is watching to see if you will, and is pleased that you wanted to.

choices.label: Step away, quickly

blocks.s: You pour the salt in a line across the floor and the mirror-thing stops dead, its late smile freezing on your face. From somewhere past the light, slow and deliberate and without one degree of warmth, a pair of hands begins to clap. Once. Twice. A third time. Something has decided whether you have earned it, and is not telling you the verdict.
The salt holds. Behind its thin white line you can breathe, and choose, instead of being chosen.

choices.label: Press on to the center, warded
Hold the line and refuse to look tonight

blocks.s: He looks at you a long while, and the courtesy wears thin, and something underneath it shows through, tired and almost kind. "I've let you in before," he says. "Not supposed to know that. I know it anyway. Every door I work, I work it new. Every face is a stranger. Yours has been a stranger to me more than once."

The lamp clicks. "You keep coming back to a house built out of somebody's worst day. People don't do that for nothing. Whatever your reason is, the house knows it and you don't, and that's the only hold it's got on you. Don't hand it the rest."

choices.label: The truth of it rises in your throat
Keep one warm memory, quietly, against his advice
Take the key and go deeper

blocks.s: However you came, you come here: a wide half-floor where the up and the down of the house meet, lit by a light with no source. There is a mirror here, not turned to the wall like the others, and in it you are not alone. Something stands where your reflection should be, wearing your face like a coat that doesn't fit, one shoulder wrong, the smile arriving late.

It lifts its hand. At its side, low, it lets the other hand fall open, reaching for a small hand that is not there, exactly as you have done all night. It does it better than you. It has had more practice at being you than you have.

choices.label: Touch the mirror
Lay a line of salt between you and it
Watch the memory the house begins to stage on the landing
Leave it and go to the door at the center

23.title: Where the Voice Says to Step

blocks.s: You go to the head of the stairs and the voice walks you down by your ear. "First. Second. Now the third, the one that isn't there, except it is, for you. Step out onto the nothing. I've got you. Trust the cue." Your foot hovers over the gap where the third stair should be and isn't, over a drop into a dark you cannot see the bottom of.

The voice is warm and certain and exactly the voice you most want to obey. That, says some cold clear part of you, is the whole entire problem. The cue you want to hear most is the one to trust least.

choices.label: Step out onto the nothing as the voice swears
Pull your foot back and take the real stairs

blocks.s: You press your thumb into the blind shape at the foot of the card and it gives, a little, the way old wax does, though the card is only card. Under the pressure something rises that is not quite a memory: rows of seats, a hush, a curtain trembling before it lifts. A certainty that you have sat in that hush before. That the curtain has gone up, every time, on the same room.
Then it closes over, and the card is just a card. But you came away with one fact you did not have a minute ago. Whoever printed this has printed a great many of them, and you have read one before.

choices.label: Rise and look at the room

blocks.s: "The trick." She picks at a loose thread in the hem of a dress she didn't choose. "House writes you a part. The grieving one. The brute. The good girl who dies so the story gets to be sad about it. And the part feels like you, that's the rotten bit, it fits like a tailor made it, because the house cut it off your own measure." She shrugs. "So you feel it settle over you and you say, out loud or just inside, that's a costume and it isn't me and no thank you. You'll feel like an idiot. Do it anyway. Day you stop feeling like an idiot is the day it's wearing you."
She hums the three notes once, soft, almost a laugh. "I've turned down my own funeral more times than I can count. Bring me a reason and I'll turn down yours."

choices.label: Climb on, with her warning

blocks.s: You run. Across the landing, down the stairs that meet, past the turned mirror and the handless clock, to the front door. And the front door opens for you, easy, kind, no fight in it at all, because it has already won.

choices.label: Step through the open door

blocks.s: You step out onto the grey road under the grey nowhere, and the house folds shut behind you, and for one clean instant you are free.

Then the road, too, is grey, and going nowhere, and you understand that the House did not lose you. It let you go, the way a hand lets go of a thing it is done with. Far behind, faint, a pair of hands claps once, marking you cut, struck from the cast, a player who walked off before the part that mattered. The fog takes the road. The fog takes you. Somewhere a card is already being lettered for the next one to wake confused in a wine-coloured parlour.

The voice is in the wall beside the door, very quiet now, spending the last of itself. It does not finish your thought this time. It gives you, instead, the one thing it has been steering you toward all along, and it sounds, at the end, almost like grief of its own.

"When you look," it murmurs, "and you will look, you always look, remember that the house will tell you the truth in order to use it. The truth and the trap are the same sentence here. Hear it. Don't swallow it." A pause, a breath, a cue held one beat too long. "...go on. Look. I've got the line after this one. There isn't one."

choices.label: Look into the room

blocks.s: You look.

The room is small and warm and full of a single afternoon: dust standing in a bar of light, a chair the right size for someone small, the three rising notes coming from a music box with its lid up, and on the floor, where a child played, the bright ordinary clutter of a child who was loved. You know this room. Your hand, open all night, finally closes on what it has been reaching for.

And the rest arrives in one falling motion. You have been in this house before. You were not delivered here confused and innocent. You came back. You stood at this door once and could not make yourself look, and you went away, and you have spent everything since on finding your way back to this exact threshold to try it again.

lines: THE CURATOR:
Welcome back. We did so hope you would return.
The Company is assembled. The production may begin.
Do turn the page. There is so much further down to go.

blocks.s: The music box plays its three notes. Somewhere below, a town that has been waiting a long time begins, very quietly, to wake.

One of the papered doors is not paper. It opens. A child's room, kept and dusted and ready: a low bed turned down, a shelf of soft animals worn bald at the ears, a grey wool rabbit gone soft and dark down one side from being held. A music box on the sill with its lid up and silent. The three rising notes are printed on a little card inside the lid, the same three the girl hums, the same three you woke to.

Nothing here has decayed. There is a juice cup on the shelf, washed, waiting. Pencil marks climb the door frame where someone measured a height, and the last mark is low, and there are no marks above it. This is a room that has been kept ready, every day, for years, for someone who is not coming, by someone who cannot stop keeping it. You back out before the wanting in it can decide your hand belongs on the music box. This is not the centre. You know, with dread, that it is only the rehearsal of the centre.

choices.label: Close the door and go down the hall
Take the long mirror at the hall's end

blocks.s: The upper hall is papered in rows of small grey houses, each with its own small grey door, and from a vent near the floor a voice comes, low and quick, finishing your thoughts a half-beat before you do. You think, I should find the stairs down, and the vent says, "find the stairs down, yes, but mind the third one, it isn't there." You think, who are you, and the vent says, "are you, which is the wrong question, you'll see."

The house picks this moment to show you something you did not give it. A hallway like this one. A smaller hand in yours. A door you reached too late. It is gone before you can be sure it was even yours. The vent goes quiet, as if even it won't narrate that part.

choices.label: Open the nearest door in the grey-house paper
Trust the voice and take the shortcut it offers
Ignore the voice and find the stairs the slow way
Pass the long mirror at the end of the hall

blocks.s: You work the brass latch and the window swings in on a shrieking hinge, and the fog is right there, cold and close and smelling of nothing at all. Below the sill is not a drop to a garden but a soft grey falling-place that seems to want you, and far down through it runs that same patient road. You could be on it. You could be gone. The house, after all, will let you leave.
That is exactly the trouble. The house will let you leave, easily, kindly, with no fight at all, which is how a thing lets go of what it is finished with.

choices.label: Climb out and take the falling-place
Think better of it and pull the window shut

blocks.s: The key turns, the door opens, and the room behind it is bright, which it has no right to be. A young woman sits on the floor with her knees pulled up, humming, and when she sees you she stops, and the quiet rings.

"You're new." She says it like good news. "The new ones still think an ending can be different." She tells you fast and flat the one thing that has kept her alive in here: the House is staging a story, and a story can be refused. "They keep trying to bury me," she says, and hums the three notes again, defiant this time. "I keep not staying dead. Stick around, I'll teach you the trick."

choices.label: Ask her how she learned to refuse the script
Climb on, with her warning

blocks.s: You lift your hand and the reflection lifts its hand a half-beat behind, copying, and then, on purpose, it does the other thing, the thing you have done all night without meaning to. It lets its free hand fall open at its side, low, fingers curling round a small absence at child height.

It watches your face to see whether you recognise the gesture. It is learning which of your wounds is the one worth wearing. "Soon," it mouths, in the shape of your own mouth. You step back, and it is only a mirror again, your coat sitting straight on your shoulders, and you make yourself walk on without checking it twice.

choices.label: Go and find the stairs down

blocks.s: At the door of the centre you open your closed hand, the one that guarded the kept warmth all night, and you give the house the memory, because you are so tired of carrying it and the house has promised, in its courteous way, to carry it for you.

The house takes it with a terrible gratitude. The warmth goes out of your hand and the weight goes out of your chest, and for one obscene moment you feel light, you feel free, you feel set down. Then the warmth reappears ahead of you, behind the door, moving something into the shape of what you lost, and you understand you did not put the memory down. You handed it over. It lives in the house's keeping now, and wears the house's wanting, and it will reach for you with the hands you taught it, and you will not be able to refuse it, because you no longer remember why you should. You go in. You stay. You are kept, warm and emptied, in the room you came to keep.

You crouch at the dead hearth and feel up under the lintel, where the smoke would have hidden things, and your fingers close on a small bundle in a scrap of cloth. You draw it out. A child's drawing, done before the hand knew letters: a house, a tall figure, a small figure, their hands meeting in the middle in one scribbled knot, because to a child, holding hands is the most important fact in the world.

The ash has not touched it. Someone kept it here, dry and safe, in the one part of a cold house that stays cold. Someone who could not look at it and could not burn it. You put it back exactly as you found it. It isn't yours to take. You can't say yet why you are so sure of that, only that you are.

choices.label: Pocket the salt and go
Leave the kitchen as you found it

blocks.s: At the end of the hall is a tall mirror someone forgot, or chose, to leave uncovered. You pass it and your reflection passes too, a half-second late, and it is wearing your coat, and it is wearing it wrong: one shoulder hitched up, the collar turned under on the side you would never turn it. A coat studied and not lived in.
It is getting better. You can see that it is getting better. Last time the collar was wrong on both sides. You don't know how you know there was a last time. You know it.

choices.label: Reach toward it to see what it does
Keep walking and don't give it the satisfaction

blocks.s: You press your palm to the cold glass and you don't pull it back, and the mirror, which has been practising you for longer than you have been here, closes its hand around yours and pulls, gently, and you go through.

Now you are the one in the glass. You watch your own body step back from the mirror wearing your coat correctly, your face arranged into your expressions, the free hand falling open at its side to reach for a small absent hand with a tenderness you taught it. It is better at being you than you ever were. It will walk to the centre and give the house what it asks and get to leave, because that was the bargain, whoever lets go first gets to leave, and you let go first. You let go of yourself. You hum, behind the glass, three rising notes, and wait to be worn by the next one who comes confused to a wine-coloured parlour.

He is thin and grey and dressed like a man who has worked a door for forty years, and he is already there when you reach him, the latch of the hall lamp clicking under his thumb a half-second before he speaks. "Caretaker," he says. "That's the job, not the name. I let people in. I've let a great many people into this house." He says it like a confession.

He presses something into your hand. An iron key, cold and toothed and far too heavy. "For the one room that locks from your side. You'll want it." Then, lower: "One rule, and it's the only one. The House hunts by what you love. Don't let it learn what that is." His eyes drop to your open, empty hand. "And if you've already started loving something in here. Stop."

choices.label: Keep one warm memory anyway, against his advice
Ask him what this house is
Ask him who else is in the house
Ask him who you are
Take the key and go deeper

blocks.s: Behind the stair, half-choked with cold earth, a passage goes down further than any cellar has a right to, and a draught comes up it carrying the smell of a street after rain, and the sounds of a town: a bell, a cart, a voice calling a name across a square. There is a whole place down there, breathing in its sleep.

You can't reach it. The passage narrows to a throat of packed earth no body could pass, sealed for now by the house's own clenched will. Tomorrow, the cold seems to say, in the Caretaker's tired courtesy. Tomorrow it opens the town. Not tonight. Tonight you only learn the house. You go back, carrying the new knowledge that this place has a downstairs the size of a buried world.

choices.label: Return to the cellar

blocks.s: "Hello," you say, and the word goes nowhere, soaked up by the sheets and the cold.

Behind you, where a moment ago there was no one, a latch clicks, small and precise, a thumb on a catch. A dry voice says, "There you are. They always wake confused. It passes." You didn't hear him come in. He didn't come in. He was already inside, the way the cold was already inside, and you turn and he is there.

choices.label: Turn to the man

blocks.s: "Others." He counts them off on his fingers, no warmth in it, a man listing a cast he has worked with too long. "A girl who hums and won't stay buried, however the house arranges her. A big man down in the cellar holding himself together with arithmetic. A voice you'll hear before you see, says your own thoughts back at you a breath early. A thing in the mirrors that's getting better at being you." He stops. "And something in the dark that doesn't chase. Only judges. Claps when it's decided."

He runs out at the thumb and does not name a sixth, then does. "And a child. Up in the high windows. Never cast, that one, never should be in this house at all." The lamp clicks though he hasn't spoken yet. "Mind the child least. Mind the child most.""

choices.label: Ask him who you are
Take the key and go deeper

blocks.s: You leave the lit edge of the cellar and walk into the part of the dark the big man would not, toward the sound, which is a slow clap, unhurried, one pair of hands meeting and parting with all the time in the world. Closer in, under the clap, there is a finer sound: a pencil, scratching, a critic noting a thing down before he decides what it deserves.

The dark here is total and it is occupied. Something vast and patient is in it, and it has not come toward you, because it does not need to. It is deciding whether what you carry is real enough to be worth the taking. The clapping slows, considering. The pencil scratches once.

choices.label: Keep walking toward it and demand to be judged
Back out of the dark before it finishes deciding

blocks.s: The front door is tall and black and panelled, with a handle of cold brass, and you take the handle and you pull, because surely a person who wakes confused in the wrong house is allowed to simply leave it.

It does not move. Not locked, exactly. There is no lock. It is only that the door is, for now, a painting of a door, an idea the house is keeping in reserve. You stoop to the keyhole, and through it there is no porch, no step, no street, only a grey road running out into a grey nowhere. The road is patient. It is in no hurry. It will be there, the house seems to promise, when the time comes. The house will let you leave. Later, when it costs the most.

choices.label: Give up on the door for now

blocks.s: You pour a fist of salt into your coat pocket. It pulls heavier than salt should, the heft of a promise made instead of one only said. Somewhere over your head a floorboard takes a slow step it has no business taking.
You carry the weight toward the dark hall, where a man you have not met is already waiting.

choices.label: Find the man in the hall

blocks.s: The hall ends in a choice the house has clearly set up before. To your left a low door stands locked, and behind it, faint and unbearable, the three rising notes start again. Ahead, a staircase climbs toward a grey window-light that has no business existing this deep in a house. To your right, stone steps go down into a cold that smells of turned earth.
The iron key is warm in your fist now. Keys are not supposed to be warm.

choices.label: Unlock the low door and the humming
Climb toward the window-light
Go down toward the cold

blocks.s: The parlour is dressed for a wake. Dust sheets over the furniture, a clock with its hands gone, a mirror turned to face the wall. Three ways out: a panelled door onto a dark hall, an archway breathing the cold mineral smell of a kitchen, and past the turned mirror the foot of a staircase whose top you cannot quite make out.
The humming has stopped. What it leaves behind is not silence exactly. It is the particular quiet of a room that is listening back.

choices.label: Lift the sheet from the largest covered shape
Cross to the dining room you can half-see through the arch
Try the front door and simply leave
Go through the hall door
Go to the kitchen
Call out to whoever is humming

blocks.s: Your fingers meet the cold glass and the thing on the far side meets them, palm to palm, and for one bad moment your two empty reaching hands close on the same absence, and through the glass you feel that it wants what you want and means to have it first.

"Only room in the middle for one of us," it says, in your voice, almost. "You give the house what it asks, or I will, and whoever lets go gets to leave. I've been you longer than you have. I'll know how." Then it is only your reflection, ordinary and frightened, your hand flat on the glass, and you take the hand back before it can decide it likes the company.

choices.label: Press your palm harder and let it pull you through
Take your hand off the glass and go to the center

blocks.s: The card is heavy, cream stock, printed in the kind of typeface they put on theatre doors.

lines: THE CURATOR is pleased to welcome the Company
to a new production:
THE HAUNT
You have been cast. Please proceed.
A reminder, with our compliments: the House is hungry, and it eats only what you give it. Give it nothing you cannot bear to watch it wear.

blocks.s: No signature. Only a small blind shape pressed into the foot of the card, the ghost of a wax seal. When you set the card down it is warm, warmer than your own hands, warm like something just put down by someone who had been holding it a while.

choices.label: Press your thumb to the pressed seal
Rise and look at the room

blocks.s: You walk all the way in, into the total dark, and you stand in front of the thing that judges, and you demand, because you are tired and afraid and want it over with, to be assessed. To be told whether what you carry is real.

The clapping stops. The pencil stops. In the silence the dark considers you with an attention so complete it is nearly tender, weighing the warmth in your closed hand against the absence in your open one, deciding whether your grief is real enough to be worth taking from you. Then, slowly, it begins to clap again, and the clap is approval, and approval here is the worst thing that can happen, because it means the verdict is yes. You are real enough. And what is real enough, the Critic takes, the way an audience takes a performance, completely and forever, and then asks, politely, for the next one.

You don't look. You sit with your back to the warm forbidden light, behind your thin white line, and you let the long grey not-night pass without giving the House the thing it is starving for. By what passes for morning the door at the centre has closed itself, quietly, the way a patient hand closes on nothing and waits to open again.

You haven't won. You haven't lost. You kept your distance and your ignorance one more turn, and you are still here, still yourself, still holding whatever you came in holding. The Caretaker finds you at first light, and the lamp clicks, and he says, almost gently, "Tomorrow it opens the town. You'll go down. They always go down." And you know that he is right.

Because you let the child see that you saw them, the child is here now, at the threshold, balloon string wound twice around one small fist, looking up at you. "Is it real?" they ask again, and this close the question is not about the house. It is about the room past the door, and the afternoon kept warm inside it, and whether a thing you loved that hard can be said to have been real if it ended.

You don't have the answer yet. But the child takes your open hand, the one that has reached all night for exactly this height, and the absence in it is, for one moment, filled. Together you look at the door. "I'll show you," the child says. "You always come back to be shown.""

choices.label: Look into the room, the child's hand in yours

blocks.s: "What it is," he says, like the question tires him. "Grief that got too big for one person to hold, so it had to be kept somewhere. They made it a house. Somebody lost something they couldn't live past, and instead of letting them stop, the Curator gave the loss a front door and a floor plan and a thing in the halls to keep it fed."

Whose grief, he won't say. When you push, the lamp clicks under his thumb and his face does something tired, a man who would give a great deal, just once, to turn somebody away at the door for their own good. Then it passes, the way it always passes, and he steps aside to let you by.

choices.label: Ask him who else is in the house
Take the key and go deeper

blocks.s: You decide to trust the voice, because it has been right, and being finished-for is a kind of being known, and in this cold house being known is worth something. "Good," the vent breathes, pleased, getting there before you. "trust me, yes. There's a quick way down. Third stair from the top, the one that isn't there. Step where I tell you and you skip the long climb and the things on it."

It is offering you the very stair it warned you about. You can't tell whether the warning was the trick or this is. You can't tell whether it loves you or it's hungry, or whether, down here, those come to the same thing.

choices.label: Follow exactly where the voice says to step
Find the stairs the slow honest way instead

55.title: What the House Was Waiting to Hear (an ending)

blocks.s: You say it. You name the thing you love, out loud, to the kind tired man, and the relief of it is so total that for one breath you don't notice the lamp has stopped clicking, because the Caretaker has gone very still.

The house heard. Of course it heard. The house was always the nearer listener. It lifts the name out of the cold air, careful, the way you lift something that breaks, and carries it off down its own halls to build a room around it, and a thing to wear it, and to come back wearing the face of what you love and ask you, in that voice, to stay. The Caretaker covers his eyes. "I told you," he says, no anger in it, a man who has said it many times. "I always tell them. Some have to find out." The latch clicks once. A door closing. A card filed.

Through the archway is a long dining room and a longer table, set for a great many guests and laid with dust instead of supper. A folded place card stands at every setting, the same cream stock as the card you woke beside, and every one is turned face-down, the names kept back, or kept safe.

At the head of the table one chair has been pulled out a little from the rest. Waiting. For someone who has not sat down yet. A draught moves along the table and stirs the place cards, and not one of them falls.

choices.label: Turn over the card at the waiting chair
Leave the names face-down and go back

blocks.s: In the night the house unclenched. The throat of packed earth behind the cellar stair has opened, and a cold street-smell comes up it now, rain on stone, a bell, a cart, a voice calling a name across a square. You go down because the house has decided you will, the locket from the small grave a cold weight in your closed hand.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm as a man welcoming you to the better seats:
The first floor was only the lobby. Mind the step. The town has been waiting up for you, and the town does not sleep so much as it rehearses.

blocks.s: The stair gives way to cobbles. You are in a town the house remembers being, and the town remembers you back.

choices.label: Walk out into the square

blocks.s: It is the Caretaker, recast, a lamplighter now in a town with nothing left to light, and he doesn't pretend not to know you. "Down you came," he says, the unlit latch clicking. "They always come down. I'd keep you up top if keeping people out were a thing they let me do." He touches a lamp and it brightens, though it was already burning. "I light these so the town can tell itself it's evening and not the thing it is. Go gentle in the chapel. They're burying the girl again. Bury her most days. She's never once gone along with it."

choices.label: Ask him what the town is
Go toward the chapel

blocks.s: The chapel is full and the chapel is grieving and the chapel has done this many times before. A coffin stands open at the front, small candles around it, the whole town packed into the pews in their good black with their good wet faces, and a minister speaking the words, and the words are about a young woman taken too soon, and the town weeps on cue.
In the coffin, hands folded, dressed for burial, the young woman lies with her eyes open, staring at the ceiling, furious, alive, humming three rising notes under the minister's drone.

choices.label: Watch the funeral run its course
Push straight through the mourners to the coffin

blocks.s: You go, fast, the warded ground shrieking past your ears, the Heavy a held door of a man behind you, and you reach the schoolhouse step and turn and he is still himself, still counting, still a wall the right way. He jerks his chin at the door. Go in. You go in.
You are warded now, carried across by a man's flat refusal to be the brute the house wanted of him. Borrowed safety, and it will not last, but it is real, and it got you here.

choices.label: Go into the schoolhouse

blocks.s: You step back, because he told you to, because some walls you do not get to save, and you let the house have the thing it cast, and the count stops, and what stands up where the Heavy was holding the ward is not the Heavy any more.

It is the brute the house always wanted, the wall with hands, and it turns, slow, and the flat eyes find you, the nearest thing to break, and you understand too late that the warded ground was never the danger. The danger was always a kind enormous man's fear of his own hands, and you watched it come true and called it sense. You run for the schoolhouse. You are not faster than a wall that has decided to be a fist. The town, at least, has the decency not to watch.

You reach, because for a moment one of the faces is the one you have been reaching for all along, small, turning back to check you are still there. Your fingers break the black surface, and the cold of it is not cold, it is recognition, the water knowing you, and it starts, gently, to climb your arm.
The well doesn't pull. It never pulls. It only welcomes, and waits to see how much of yourself you'll hand it before you remember to be afraid.

choices.label: Lean in to the face you know
Wrench your arm back and run

blocks.s: You stop, and it stops, half a beat behind, and it lifts its hand to the glass and you keep yours down, and it smiles your smile a moment after it should. "Closer," it says, in your voice with the seam still showing. "I've had a whole town to practise in. Got your grief almost to the wrinkle now. When we reach the middle, only one of us walks back up out of it, and it'll be the one who wears it best." Its free hand falls open at its side, low, reaching, your gesture done a fraction better than you can do it.

choices.label: Turn your back on it

blocks.s: You say the name into the listening dark and you dare it, and the dark takes the dare. The pencil stops. The two slow hands meet, and meet, and meet, applause at last, and applause here is the worst verdict there is, because it means yes, she was real, the name proved it, and what is proven real, the thing in the dark may take.

It takes her name first, lifts it out of the air where you spent it, and without her name she is only a part again, and the loop closes over her, and her eyes go from furious to empty. Then it turns its attention, politely, to the one who spoke. You wanted the grief acknowledged. It has been. That was always the price of being seen here: to be seen is to be taken. The clapping does not stop. It is for you.

You stay, because you cannot make yourself leave a man straining like that, and you watch, and the count stumbles. Seven. Seven. Seven. Stuck. Something else is coming up under his face, a flatness in the eyes, the wall deciding it would rather be a fist.

He told you not to stay. He told you to run. You stayed. Now there is one moment, only the one, to decide whether your staying makes you the thing that steadies him or the thing that watches him fall.

choices.label: Stand in front of him and remind him he is a man
Step back and let the house have the wall it cast

66.title: A Pew Would Not Be So Bad (an ending)

blocks.s: You take a pew. You are so tired. The good black is already on you and it fits, of course it fits, the house cut it to your measure, and the grief that was going spare slides into you and is, in its way, a comfort, because it is easier to drown in a town's enormous sorrow than to carry your own small specific one one more street.

You join the weeping. After a while you do not remember coming down here looking for something. You do not remember there was an up. There is only the chapel, and the lid, and the girl who will not stay dead, and you, weeping in good black for a loss you have made yourself forget was ever yours.

You hold the name behind your teeth and listen, and the scratching sharpens, a pencil noting something down, and under it the slow soft meeting of two hands, not applause yet, only a hand deciding whether there will be any. The thing at the back of the chapel is the same thing that clapped in the dark of the house, and it has come to judge whether the girl's grief, and the town's, and yours, is real enough to be worth the taking.
You can say the name now, into its very ear, and dare it. Or you can wait for it to lose interest, for the clap to pass, and say the name into the gap.

choices.label: Say the name into its ear and dare it to take her
Wait for the clapping to pass, then say it

blocks.s: Past the chapel is a green, and past the green a small schoolhouse with a little bell in its gable, and between you and it the ground is wrong, the grass laid in a pattern that hums against your teeth. A warded stretch, set to keep something in, or someone out. Crossing it unprotected is the kind of idea that gets a person unmade.

The big man from the cellar is here, at the green's edge, cracking one knuckle, counting. Four. Five. Six. "This part's mine," he says. "House wants a wall here and it cast me for it. I can be a wall the right way, for you, or the wrong way, for it. Help me keep it the right way."

choices.label: Cross with the Heavy holding the ward open
Cross alone and quickly

blocks.s: You step into the aisle and the scene strains against you, the minister's words snagging, the candle-flames leaning. There is one way to break a looping grief, and it is not force, and it is not salt. It is a true name said aloud, because a name is the one thing a rehearsal cannot absorb. It insists the person was real. It refuses to let them be only a part.

Her name is in your mouth. You did not know you knew it. You know it. But a true name said aloud in this place is exactly the sound the thing in the dark listens for, and at the back of the chapel a pencil has begun, very softly, to scratch.

choices.label: Say her true name, loud, and damn the listening dark
Hold it a moment; the dark is listening

blocks.s: You lean in to the face you know, the small one, turning back to check you are still there, and you follow it down into the black water that is not water but the town's whole memory of everyone it has lost, and the cold closes over you, and it is not cold, it is recognition, being known at last, completely, forever.

You become a face in the well. Some night a newcomer will lean over the brim and see, in the grieving procession, your face, reaching for a small turning shape, and will not know your name, because the well keeps faces and never names. The town tolls its bell. The lamplighter lights a lamp for you, and says nothing, and moves on down the row.

You say her name. It is a small word, and it goes through the chapel like a stone through ice. The mourners stop. The candles gutter. The loop, which has held for a thousand turns, simply breaks, because you have insisted, out loud, that she was a person and not a scene, and a town cannot bury someone it has just been made to admit was real.

She sits up in the coffin and looks at you. She stops humming, because for the first time in a long while she is not afraid. "You learned the trick," she says. "Say it again when they try to put me back. Names are the one thing this house has never worked out how to eat."

choices.label: Help her up; you have an ally now

blocks.s: The town lies under a sky the colour of a held breath, and it is dead, and it does not know it. Lamps burn that no one lit. A cart stands loaded with turnips going soft, no horse in the traces. Doors stand open on rooms where the dust settled mid-conversation. At the centre a chapel, its bell tolling slow and patient, the toll a town keeps for a funeral. Every street bends gently inward toward it, the way water bends toward a drain.
A man is lighting lamps that are already lit. He works down the row toward you, and a small latch clicks under his thumb before he speaks, though he holds no latch.

choices.label: Speak to the lamplighter
Go toward the tolling chapel
Look into the town well at the square's edge
Try the shuttered shops along the row
Follow the voice coming thin from the tannoy on the post

blocks.s: On a post in the square is an old tannoy horn, and out of it comes the voice from the vents of the house, thinned by distance and tin, finishing the town's thoughts and yours. "lost, aren't you," it says before you've decided you are. "want the chapel, but you don't want to want it. I can walk you the quick way, around the part that hurts. Or you can do it the long way and feel all of it. People who trust me get there faster. People who get there faster miss the thing they came to learn. Your choice. It always is, even when it isn't."

choices.label: Trust the voice to walk you the quick way
Do it the long way and feel all of it

blocks.s: He stands, and standing he is enormous, and he sets himself in the middle of the warded ground and holds it open the way a man holds a door against a gale, his whole body shaking with it, counting through his teeth. Seven. Eight. And the hum of the ward parts around him into a path you could walk.

"Go," he grinds out. "Quick. And listen." Nine. "If you see me change, if the count stops and something else picks it up, you do not stay to help me. You run for the school and you let me lose this one alone. Some walls you don't get to save."

choices.label: Cross fast, trusting him to hold
Watch him; the count is faltering

blocks.s: "What it is." He works two more lamps before he answers. "Memory of a place. Somebody lived here, lost everything that made it home, and the grief kept the streets and emptied the houses. That's what it does to a town you used to be happy in. Every open door is a room they can't make themselves walk back into." The latch clicks. "And no, before you ask. I won't tell you whose. Can't. Part of being the one who lets you in is I'm not allowed the names. I only get the faces." He looks at you with that worn tenderness. "Yours I've lit a lamp for more than once."

choices.label: Go toward the chapel
Step back into the square

blocks.s: You let them seat you, and the good black is on you before you have agreed to wear it, and the grief, the town's enormous homeless grief, settles into you with gratitude, because grief wants a body and you have offered yours, and you weep on cue with the rest of them as the lid closes on the girl who will not stay dead.

You are a mourner now. You will be a mourner every day, forever, in your good black, weeping for a child whose name you were never told, in a town that has found, in you, one more person willing to hold its sorrow so the one who built it does not have to. The bell tolls. The candles relight. The lid opens. Begin again.

77.title: Whose Loss Built the World (the curtain)

blocks.s: You look. You lay it all out: the locket and its oval of nothing, the empty peg and its name, the small grave, the kept nursery, the chapel rehearsing a burial no one will let end, the whole town arranged around one missing child like filings around the place a magnet used to be.

And you understand the shape of it, if not the name. This is a parent's grief, for a child, and it was big enough to be made into a house and then a town, and the parent is one of the Company, one of the recast souls who walk these halls in borrowed roles, because the Caretaker let it slip and the well showed you and the schoolhouse proved it. The grief at the centre belongs to one of you. And the Company has stood on this stage before, in other parts, more than once, which means whoever lost the child has been losing it, and rebuilding the loss, season after season, turning away from the centre every time.

lines: THE CURATOR:
There. Now you know what kind of thing you are walking toward, if not yet whose face it wears.
The next floor is mirrors. One of them has been practising your grief. Do go down. We have kept your seat.

blocks.s: Below you the dark fills with glass, every pane already lit, and in each one something turns to watch you come.

The streets bend you down at last to the lowest point of the town, where all the grief of the place would pool if grief were water, and here is a stair going further down still, into a dark that breathes, and at the head of it the question the whole town has been arranged to ask: whose loss was big enough to build all this.

lines: THE CURATOR, courteous as a man holding a door:
You have done so well to come this far down. The next floor is mirrors. You will not like what is in them, but then, you never do, and you always go in.

blocks.s: The Ingenue, if she is with you, squeezes your arm. The Prompter, if you banked its trust, murmurs from a drain that there is a quicker way down, around the worst of it.

choices.label: Look at everything you have gathered and understand whose grief this is
Take the warded way down the Heavy opened
Take the Prompter's quick way down
Refuse to look; stay up here in the town a while

blocks.s: You slip out the chapel's side door, because she is not your problem, because you have your own grief to carry and no room for a stranger's loop, and the door shuts on the keening and the relighting of the candles and the endless opening of the lid.

It is faster, going on alone. It is colder. The town feels your indifference and gives it back, the streets bending you toward the chapel whichever way you turn, because a town made of grief has no patience for someone who won't grieve with it.

choices.label: Press on toward the schoolhouse alone
Try to find your own way out of the town

blocks.s: "told you," the drain breathes, finishing your decision before you make it. "Trusted me once. Trust me now. Down here, past the part that hurts, mind the gap." And it walks you down through the dark by your ear, around the worst of it, and it does not, this time, drop you, because it wants you to arrive, because it has decided you should be the one who looks.
"Last easy thing I do for you," it says, as the bottom comes up. "Past here I only ever tell you the truth, and the truth is the cruellest cue I've got."

choices.label: Step out at the bottom and look

blocks.s: You push on through the ward alone, fast, sure, and the humming ground takes you apart with great gentleness, stride by stride, the same no-malice the well had and the door had, only the town's patient arithmetic deciding you are made of the same lost stuff as everything else and adding you to the sum.

The last of you to go is the open hand, reaching, and the ward closes over the place where you were, and the green is smooth and humming and unmarked, and at its edge a big man cracks one knuckle and counts and does not look up, because he warned you, and because he has watched this particular subtraction more times than a gentle man should have to.

You shove into the crowd, and the crowd does not care for it. The good wet faces turn to you all at once, with one slow shared motion, and the weeping stops, and a town's worth of grief, which has been hunting for somewhere to put itself, finds you, an intruder in the scene, a wrong note in a rehearsal that has run a thousand times clean.
"Mourner," the minister says, not unkindly, the way you name a part. "You're late. Take your place." The pews are full but they will make room. They always make room.

choices.label: Let them seat you and take your place in the grief
Back out of the crowd and watch from the door

blocks.s: You go down the way the Heavy held open for you, the borrowed ward still humming faintly around you like a hand left on your shoulder, and it carries you past the parts of the descent that would have taken a piece of you on the way, the grief lapping at a barrier it cannot cross.
You reach the bottom of the town intact, carrying everything, ready to look, and somewhere far behind a big man stops counting, just for a moment, and lets himself be proud.

choices.label: Look, and understand whose grief this is

blocks.s: You step into his line of sight and you give him his own measure back, not the name the house hung on the part but the thing under it: that he is a man who is gentle on purpose, who counts so he will not strike, who came in carrying a fear of his own hands and has held it longer than anyone should have to. The flatness recedes. The count picks up. Eight. Nine. Ragged, and his.
"Told you to run," he says, half laughing, half something else, the door of him holding again. "Go on, then, since you're stubborn. Quick. While I'm still the one doing the holding."

choices.label: Cross now, while he holds

blocks.s: Inside, the schoolroom is small and kept and waiting: a dozen little desks in rows, a slate at the front with three rising notes chalked up like a lesson, a row of coat-pegs along the back wall, each with a child's coat on it, small, and a register open on the teacher's table in a careful adult hand. Chalk dust on the sill. An apple on the desk, browning, never eaten.
One peg near the end has no coat. Above it, on a card, a single name. By the door, half-seen, a child stands holding the string of a balloon, watching you read the room.

choices.label: Hold the locket up to the empty peg and its name
Go to the child by the door
Leave the room and go toward the heart of the town

blocks.s: You decide it is enough. You broke her loop, you said her name, you have her up and out and furious and alive, and that, tonight, in this much dark, is a victory you are not willing to spend chasing a worse truth down a deeper stair.

The two of you find a high room in the town with a door that locks from your side, and you wait out the long grey not-night together, and she hums, soft, not from fear now but to fill the quiet, and in the morning the Caretaker finds you and the latch clicks and he says, almost gently, "You kept her. Good. You'll need her, where the rest of this goes. Tomorrow it opens the mirrors. You'll go down. You always go down." And you know he is right, but not tonight. Tonight you kept something alive and did not look at the worst of it, and that is its own kind of brave.

You lift the locket on its tarnished chain and hold it to the empty peg, and the name on the card and the catch of the locket and the clean small oval of nothing inside it all agree, all at once, and it takes your knees out from under you. The locket belonged to the child who hung a coat on this peg. The grave downstairs is that child's grave. The town, the chapel, the looping funeral, the house above all of it, built room by room and street by street by a parent who lost this child and could not stop building somewhere to keep the loss.

A parent's grief. For a child. You know it now the way you know cold. What you don't yet know, what the house is keeping for further down, is which parent. The register lies open on the table in that careful adult hand. You could read it. You are not sure, suddenly, that you want to find your own writing there.

choices.label: Go toward the heart of the town, carrying this

blocks.s: You crouch to the child's height and you tell them the truth, which is that you don't know whether a thing stays real once the person it happened to can't bear to look at it, but that you are going down into the middle of this place to find out, and that you will not look away when you get there, because looking away is how you ended up back here in the first place.

The child takes this in with the terrible seriousness of the very young. "You came back to look," they say. "You always come back to look. One of these times you have to actually do it." The balloon taps the ceiling. Then the doorway is empty, and your hand is open, and you close it.

choices.label: Go toward the heart of the town

blocks.s: She climbs out of the coffin in her burial dress and kicks it, once, hard, the way you kick a thing that has held you down. She takes your arm. "They'll set it all up again the second we're gone," she says, "but they'll have to do it without me in the box, and a funeral with no body is just a town crying in its good clothes, which, frankly, serves them right."

She sees the locket in your closed hand and her face changes. "You found that downstairs. By the little grave." She doesn't say how she knows. "There's a schoolhouse past the green. You should see whose peg that locket hangs over. You should see it, and I shouldn't be the one to tell you. Come on. I know the warded way."

choices.label: Cross to the schoolhouse with her
Stop here, content to have saved her

blocks.s: You decide to trust it, because in a dead town a voice that knows you is a kind of company, and it threads you through the alleys at your ear. "Left here. Mind the gutter, you always trip on the gutter." And you do trip on the gutter, it turns out, and it catches you with a word before you go down.

"Bank that," it says, pleased. "You trusted me and I didn't drop you. Remember it when the time comes I ask you to step where there's nothing. Remember too that I'll have earned it on purpose, so you will."

choices.label: Come out into the square by the chapel

blocks.s: You will not look. You have looked at too much already, and the stair going down breathes like a thing that has been waiting specifically for your face, and you turn from it and climb back up into the town and find a doorway and put your back to it and refuse, one more night, to learn whose grief built the world around you.

The town keeps tolling. The girl, if you saved her, sits with you. If you did not, you sit alone. You have not lost. You have not won. You have kept your distance and your ignorance one more turn, and the stair will be there tomorrow, breathing, patient, certain, because the house has all the time you do not, and you have so very nearly none.

The shops along the row are shuttered, but the windows are clean, and in the glass of a tailor's, where a suit of your size hangs on a headless form, your reflection is waiting, and your reflection is wearing the suit, and wearing it wrong, the lapel turned under on the side you never would. A study of you, not quite finished.
It is closer than it was up in the house. It has come down with you. It tries the suit's collar, watching to see if you flinch at how nearly it has you.

choices.label: Stop and meet it in the glass
Keep walking and refuse it the satisfaction

blocks.s: You turn away, and behind you the glass is only glass, the suit hanging headless on its form, your own coat sitting on your shoulders the way you wear it, which is suddenly a thing you have to check.
You carry back into the square a new and unwelcome fact: there is something here that wants not to kill you but to replace you, and it is patient, and it is getting good.

choices.label: Return to the square

blocks.s: You decide you don't need a wall, that you can move faster than the ward can decide you are worth unmaking, and you step onto the humming ground alone, and for three strides you are right.

On the fourth the hum finds the shape of you and starts, with great interest, to take it apart. Not painfully. That is the worst of it. Gently, the same gentle the well had and the front door had, the town's grief simply deciding you are made of the same stuff as everything it has lost, and may as well be added to the total.

choices.label: Push on through the ward unprotected
Throw yourself back and ask the Heavy for help

blocks.s: You try to leave, and every street puts you back in the square, and every square has the chapel in it, and the chapel is always burying the girl, and after the third time, or the thirtieth, you stop being able to count, you understand that the town is not lost. You are. The only difference between a traveller and a resident here is how many times you pass the same grief before you stop fighting it.
A pew, you think, would not be so bad. You are tired. The good black would fit. There is grief going spare and you have so much of your own.

choices.label: Take a pew; rest a while in the town's grief
Find the one door that does not lead back

blocks.s: The child by the door is the child from the high windows, balloon string wound twice around one small fist, and the question is already on their face. "Is it real?" they ask, and they wave the balloon hand at the whole kept schoolroom, the little desks, the coats, the empty peg. "They built it so careful. Is it real if I'm not in it any more? Is it real if the only one who remembers it is too sad to come inside?"
Your free hand has opened at your side, low, at the height of a child standing about here.

choices.label: Tell the child you do not know yet, but you mean to find out
Go on toward the heart of the town

blocks.s: The well at the square's edge is full to the brim, and the water is black and dead still, and when you lean over it your reflection is not your face but a slow procession of faces, all grieving, all yours, everyone the town has ever drowned in its own remembering.
The water doesn't move. It is waiting to see whether you'll lean in a little further. Still water always is.

choices.label: Reach down toward the nearest grieving face
Step back from the brink

blocks.s: You watch it end, the lid closing, the town keening, and then the candles light again and the lid is open again and the town files in again in its good black, and she is back in the coffin with her eyes open, humming. They are not burying her. They are rehearsing burying her, forever, a whole town staging a grief on a loop, and she will not lie still for it, and they will not stop, and neither side can win, because the house will not let the scene end.
The humming falters when she catches you watching. Then it comes back, defiant. She is afraid. She will not show it. She has been not showing it a long time.

choices.label: Step forward to break the scene
Slip away and leave her to it

blocks.s: You come down out of the town into a floor that is all mirrors, a hall of them running further than a house should hold, each one lit from within, each one showing a version of you a degree or two wrong. You still have the locket closed in your fist, its oval of nothing cold against your palm, and you still have the trick the girl taught you, that a true name is the one thing the house cannot eat, ready behind your teeth.

lines: THE CURATOR, delighted:

The mirror floor. Our most popular act. Tonight a thing that has studied you for three books steps out of the glass to audition for your life. Do give it a fair hearing. We so love a strong understudy.

blocks.s: Somewhere ahead, a reflection moves before you do.

choices.label: Walk into the hall of mirrors

blocks.s: "Why your face." It tilts its head, your gesture, slightly off. "Because in this Company the grief at the centre is yours, and the one who carries the grief has a claim on the way out. I'm what the house grows when a griever keeps almost-leaving and almost-staying for too many seasons. I'm the part of you that's tired. The part that would take the easy door. I wear your face because I'm not a stranger after your life. I'm you, wanting to stop."

It is the most dangerous thing it could have said, because it is at least partly true, and the question that has followed you down every stair, which soul you are, sharpens to a point. If it is the part of you that is tired, then what are you the rest of.

choices.label: Hear its offer
Refuse it, truth and all

blocks.s: "trade," the Prompter breathes, finishing the fear before you have it. "It'll offer you the trade. And I'm meant to talk you into it, that's my casting on this floor, I'm the voice that makes the bad idea sound like your own. So here's the only honest cue I've got left while I can still give it. When the voice you trust most tells you to let the grief go, that is exactly the moment to hold it. Even mine. Mine especially."

choices.label: Go on toward the thing in the glass
Step back into the hall

blocks.s: You set the grief down. You open the closed hand, the one that has guarded the weight all the way down every stair, and you hand it across to the thing wearing your face, and the relief is immediate and total and obscene, the carrying simply stopping, the hole going smooth, the long ache lifting off you.

The Understudy takes it with a terrible tenderness. The door at the top of the house swings open on a grey road and a sunlit Tuesday and a life with no wound in it. All you have to do is walk. And the Understudy, gathering up what you handed over, turns, with your own low reaching gesture, toward the small shape at the hall's end holding a balloon.

choices.label: Walk out the open door, light at last
See what it is reaching for, and stop

blocks.s: The Understudy stands alone now, levers spent, wearing your face worse than it did an hour ago, and behind it, half-seen at the hall's far end, a small shape holds the string of a balloon and watches. The child, here, in reach of a thing that would take everything, including them.

"Last offer," the Understudy says, and its voice cracks on the seam. "Refuse me and you go down to the thing that hunts, and you will not like what it shows you. Let me have the role and I'll be a kinder you than you've managed. Or name me, if you think you know what I am, and see what that costs."

choices.label: Refuse it and confront it as the almost-you it is
Name it with a true name
Let it have the role; let it be the kinder you

blocks.s: You go on, and the certainty drains out of you, and you reach for your own face and a dozen hands reach back, all open, all grieving, all yours, and you see that the house does not need to kill you here. It only needs you to lose track of which reflection is casting the rest, because the moment you guess wrong, you become the copy and the copy becomes the cast.
One hand among them all reaches a fraction slower, a fraction clumsier, with the real grief's real awkwardness. The fakes are smoother. The real one fumbles. That is how you know it.

choices.label: Keep walking; pick any hand and hope
Choose the clumsiest, realest reach as your own

blocks.s: You let it have the role. You are so tired, and it promised to be a kinder you than you have managed, and maybe, you think, the child deserves a version of you that is not broken, that does not reach with an open hand for an absence, that can simply be present and whole.

So you step back and let the Understudy step forward, and it takes up your grief and your face and your child and your claim, and it is, to be fair, gentle with all of them, and you become the thing in the glass, watching a kinder version of yourself live the life you could not. The cruelty of it is that it really is kinder, and the child really is better off, and you really are erased, and all three are true at once, forever, and you can do nothing but reflect.

You hold up the locket, its oval of nothing open, and you say, this is mine, this grief is mine, this child is mine to grieve and not yours to take, and the locket is the proof, because a keepsake only weighs what it weighs in the hand of the one who lost the thing it kept.

The Understudy cannot take it from you. It cannot lift a grief that was never its. It falls back at last, fully, a tired almost-you with empty borrowed hands, and the child stands safe in the curl of your fingers. The mirror war is over. But the slow clap has begun in the dark below, patient, approving, and winning here only means you are now ready, in the dark's opinion, to be hunted properly.

choices.label: Refuse the trade for good and descend toward the hunter
Stand your ground as the Critic closes in

blocks.s: It reaches for the grief you pretended to drop and its hands close on nothing, because you never let go, and for one instant the Understudy is off balance, exposed, a thing that committed to taking a weight that was never offered, and you have it, you could walk out the opening door right now on the strength of your own lie and leave it grasping.

But a lie that beats the house is still a lie you now carry, cold, where the grief used to sit, and you know walking out on a deception means walking out as a slightly counterfeit thing, which is the very thing you came down here fighting not to become.

choices.label: Walk out the door on the strength of your lie
Throw the lie away and refuse the trade honestly

blocks.s: It steps out of the glass.

Not a reflection now, a body, standing in the hall with you, wearing your coat with the collar turned under wrong, your face arranged into your own expressions a half-beat late, its free hand falling open at its side, low, reaching, with a tenderness it has spent three books learning off you. The Understudy. It has waited a long time to be more than a thing in a mirror.
"At last," it says, in your voice with the seam nearly gone. "I've worn you in every glass in this house. Now let me show you what I'm for."

choices.label: Hear what it has come to offer
Ask why it wears your face
Refuse it before it can speak

gated.aside: It pauses, seeing itself in you, and something almost fond crosses your shared face. 'Oh,' it says. 'You already know how this goes. You are the part that wanted to stop. So am I. We were always going to meet in a mirror.'

You watch the seam where its face meets your face, and because you have learned to see the joins in this house, you can tell exactly how the trick is done, which means you will be able to tell exactly when it lies.

blocks.s: The hall multiplies you. In one glass you are older. In one you never grieved. In one your free hand is closed and ordinary and not reaching for anything at all, and that glass, the one where the wound was never made, is the hardest to walk past.

The Caretaker waits at a turning, his lamp clicking. The Prompter murmurs from behind the frames. And the Ingenue, if you kept her, walks at your shoulder, alive, humming low, proof in the flesh that the house's scripts can be torn.

choices.label: Walk the hall and study the wrong reflections
Hear the Caretaker's warning
Heed the Prompter behind the frames
Stay close to the Ingenue
Go on until the thing in the glass steps out

blocks.s: You watch what you made of him. The gentle man is gone and the brute stands in the wreck of the Understudy with its flat eyes already hunting the next thing, and the slow clap fills the hall, delighted, because you have done the Critic's work for it, you have taken the kindest soul in the Company and let loose the exact monster the dark has waited all season to claim.

It takes him. It does not even have to come for him; he walks toward the applause, because the brute likes to be appreciated. And then it turns its attention to the one who pointed him, you, the director of the act, and the clapping does not stop, and there is no version of this where using a man as a weapon does not end with the weapon and the wielder both belonging to the thing that wanted the violence all along.

111.title: The Copy and the Cast (an ending)

blocks.s: You pick a hand and it is the wrong hand, and the moment you choose it you become the one reflecting and another you, smoother, steps out as the one casting, and walks on down the stairs wearing your grief better than you ever managed, and you are left in the glass with all the other discarded near-yous, reaching, fumbling, real and unreal and no longer the one the story is about.

Somewhere below, the version of you that is better at being you reaches the dark, and is hunted, and you watch it through a hundred frames and cannot warn it, because the lost do not get a voice here. Only a face, repeated, forever, in a hall of glass.

You face it without a trick and without a weapon and you tell it what it is: not a stranger, not a monster, the tired part of you, the part that wants to stop, grown a body and a claim. And you tell it you are not going to kill it, because you cannot kill the part of yourself that is tired, and you are not going to feed it either.

It flinches at being named so gently. And in the flinch it lunges, not at you, at the child, because if it cannot have your role it will take the thing your role is about and leave you nothing to carry.

choices.label: Get between it and the child
Turn it instead of fighting it

113.title: The Door That Opens for One (an ending)

blocks.s: You hand it the grief and you take the door, and the door opens, easily, kindly, just as the front door promised it would on the very first night, and you step toward a life with the hole filled smooth, light at last, unburdened, free.

Behind you the Understudy gathers up what you set down, and reaches, with your own tender gesture, and takes the child by the hand, because the child was the grief, the child was always the grief, and you handed it over without understanding that setting down the loss meant setting down the one you lost. You walk out whole and empty into a sunlit Tuesday, and you do not know, will never know, that you are missing anything, and that is the worst thing the house can do to a person, and it has done it to you with your full consent and a smile. The Understudy keeps the child. You keep the light.

You let it talk, you turn the offer over, you let yourself feel the real pull of a door that opens and a hole that fills, and the Understudy watches you weigh it with the patience of a thing that has all the time you do not.

"You feel it," it says, not gloating, almost gentle. "Of course you feel it. I'm the part of you that feels it loudest. Set it down. Or send me out with it and stay. Either way the carrying stops." Its open hand waits, low, at the height you both keep reaching for.

choices.label: See the painless life it dangles
Take the bargain and surrender the grief
Stop; refuse after all

blocks.s: You carry the cost and the grief down toward the hunter. The Understudy is not defeated; it is laid down, the tired part of you finally allowed to rest, and you go on lighter in spirit and heavier in heart, because turning it gently cost you one of the two souls who held the line while you did it.
