You don't yet know which. You'll find out below, in the dark, when you reach for an ally and your hand closes on the place where they were. The mirror floor falls away behind you, its hundred wrong reflections going dark one by one, and ahead the slow clap waits, patient, and the child walks beside you holding a balloon, and you have kept your grief and your child and your self, and paid for it with a friend, which is, the house seems to murmur, the most honest price it has charged you yet.

"Listen, because I don't get to say this often and mean it," the Caretaker says, the latch clicking fast. "The house is going to offer you a trade on this floor. It'll sound like mercy. Give up the grief, it'll say, and the door opens, and you walk out into a life with the hole filled in." The latch clicks. "Whatever it offers, the price is never printed on the front of the card. I've let people take that trade. I've watched what walks out wearing their face after. It is not them. Refuse it. Refuse it even though refusing means going further down."

choices.label: Go on toward the thing in the glass
Step back into the hall

blocks.s: You reach for the painless life, because you are so tired, because the open hand has been reaching for an absence so long it would take anything offered, and the Understudy smiles your smile and opens its arms to take the weight you are setting down.

choices.label: Hand it the grief and take the door

blocks.s: You use the trick. You say a true name at it, the way the girl said a town quiet, except the name you say is the one thing the Understudy cannot wear: not a name it learned off you, but the name of the one you lost, the small specific true name the whole house is built around and has never once been allowed to be spoken inside.

The hall rings. The Understudy staggers, because you have just insisted, out loud, in the deepest part of the house, that the loss is real and is yours and has a name, and a counterfeit cannot stand in the presence of a thing that true. But the dark heard the name too. The pencil, somewhere, is writing it down.

choices.label: Shield the child with the real keepsake before the dark moves
Drive the staggered Understudy off

blocks.s: The Heavy stands at the hall's end, holding himself the way he always does, cracking one knuckle, counting, but the count is wrong tonight, too fast, four-five-six-seven stumbling into each other, because the Understudy is at his ear pouring the old poison: that he is a wall, that he is a weapon, that the simple way to win the mirror war is to let the big man be the brute the house has always wanted and point him at the problem.
He looks at you over the Understudy's shoulder, eyes flat and starting to go flatter. "Tell me what I am. Quick. Before it tells me first."

choices.label: Remind him he is a man who counts so he will not strike
Use him; point the weapon at the Understudy

blocks.s: You refuse. Not cleverly, not with a trick, just plainly: no. You will not set the grief down and you will not send it out the door in your place. The grief is the last true thing you have, the proof that what you lost was real and was yours and mattered, and a you without it would be exactly the smooth untouched stranger in the glass, which is to say not you at all.

The Understudy's borrowed face does something complicated. Disappointment. And under it, almost, relief. "Then we do it the hard way," it says. "The house has one more lever, and his name is the big man, and it's already pulling him."

choices.label: Go to the Heavy

blocks.s: "Here's the trade," it says, and the Curator's courtesy is in its borrowed mouth now. "The house wants the grief. It has only ever wanted the grief. Give it up, hand it over, set down the loss you've carried down every stair, and the door at the top opens, and you walk out into the world with the hole in you filled smooth."

It spreads your hands. "But the door opens for one. So either you take it, and I keep what you set down. Or I take it, and you keep the grief and stay. One of us leaves light. One stays heavy. Choose."

choices.label: Refuse the trade outright
Entertain it; ask what 'setting it down' really means
Pretend to accept, and trick it

gated.aside: You feel the pull of the offer in your closed hand, in the white of your own knuckles. You, of all of them, want to set it down. That is precisely why you must not.
Read the back of the card. The price is always printed on the back. Set the grief down and you set down the one you came for.

choice.label: You have already seen the lie. Name the offer for the trick it is.

blocks.s: It shows you, because it knows you, the glass to your left clouding and clearing on a life: a morning, ordinary, sunlit, you in it whole and easy and untouched by the thing that happened. No open hand reaching for an absent height. No wound. No stairs. No house. A person who was never broken walking through a Tuesday like it was nothing, because to them it was.
"That," the Understudy says softly, "is what setting it down buys. Not forgetting. Better than forgetting. Never having had the wound at all. You could be that. You only have to hand me the rest."

choices.label: Turn from the painless life and refuse
Reach for the Tuesday where you were never broken

blocks.s: You throw yourself across the hall and get between the Understudy and the child, and the small hand finds your open one and holds, and the absence you have reached for down every stair is, for one breath, filled, warm, real.

The Understudy claws at you to get past, and you have a heartbeat to decide how to hold the line: with the locket, the true keepsake, the one thing in this house that proves whose child this is, or with nothing but your body and your refusal, which may not be enough against a thing wearing your own strength.

choices.label: Hold up the locket and prove whose grief this is
Hold with only your body; you are too slow

124.title: The Verdict on the Trade (an ending)

blocks.s: You stand and let it judge you. The pencil stops. The two slow hands meet, and meet, and meet, and the verdict is the one the dark always gives a thing that has proven itself real: approval, which is appetite. You survived the mirror war. You kept the grief. You held the child. You did all of it so well that you are now, in the dark's assessment, the realest thing on the floor, and the realest thing is what it takes.

It is not ready to be seen, so you never see it. You only feel the assessment close over you like a verdict read in a language of clapping, and the last thing you know is the child's hand slipping out of yours as the dark files you both under taken, and the pencil, somewhere, turning to a fresh page for the next one to come down and be real enough to lose.

You say it fast and you say it true: that he is a man who is gentle on purpose, who counts so he will not strike, who has carried a fear of his own hands down every stair and never once let it loose, and that you would rather lose the mirror war with him a man than win it with him a weapon.

The count steadies. Eight. Nine. His. The flatness drains back out of his eyes, and he shrugs the Understudy off him like a wet coat, and the Understudy staggers, its lever denied, and the two of you turn to face it together, the right way.

choices.label: Face the Understudy, the Heavy beside you

blocks.s: You walk the hall slowly, and the reflections drift further from you the deeper you go, until you are surrounded by a hundred near-yous all moving a half-beat off, and the trouble starts when you can't remember which way you turned, and two of the reflections turn the other way, and one is leading and one is following and you have lost the thread of which one is casting the rest.

Your own face is in here somewhere. The reliable way to find it is the wound. Only the real one reaches with the open hand for the small absent height. The copies have all learned to fake it, and they fake it a fraction too well.

choices.label: Trust the open hand and find your own face
Press on, no longer sure which one is you

blocks.s: You do not fight it and you do not flee it. You turn it, the way you turn an understudy who has forgotten they are not the lead: you take its borrowed reaching hand in yours and you let it feel, through the touch, that the grief it wants is not a prize but a weight, that carrying it is not the privilege it imagined but the thing that has been breaking you for seasons, and that it does not actually want this, it only wanted to stop, and there are gentler ways to stop than stealing a life.

It comes apart quietly, not defeated, released, a tired part of you laid down at last. But turning it took everything you had, and you feel the cost land somewhere behind you, on the Heavy holding the dark, or the Ingenue holding the line, one of them spent so that this could be gentle.

choices.label: Carry the cost and the grief down toward the hunter

blocks.s: You do the easy thing. You point him. You say the word that lets the wall be a fist, and the Heavy's count stops, and what comes up behind his face is the brute the house cut from his measure, enormous and flat-eyed and free at last of the arithmetic that leashed it, and it falls on the Understudy with a sound you will hear for a long time.

It works. Of course it works. That is the horror of the easy thing. And when the Understudy is dealt with, the brute that was a gentle man turns, slow, looking for the next thing to put its hands through, and the slow clap starts somewhere in the dark, delighted, because you have just built the Critic exactly the monster it has been waiting all season to take.

choices.label: Watch what you have made of him
Throw yourself between him and the dark; call him back

blocks.s: You refuse the trade, for good, and you keep the grief and the child and yourself, and you turn from the opening door and the painless Tuesday and walk the other way, toward the dark and the slow clap and whatever does the hunting down there, because the girl was right. There is always another ending. It is just always the harder one to reach.

The Understudy, laid bare as the almost-you it is, watches you go with your own face, and the question it sharpened follows you down: if it is the part of you that is tired, the part that would have taken the door, then you are the part that would not, and the house has treated you as the griever at the centre since the first cold parlour, which means the grief is not just yours to carry but yours to have caused, and you are descending, now, toward finding out exactly how.

lines: THE CURATOR:

You refused. You always refuse, in the end, which is why we keep casting you. Down you go, to the one who decides whether your dead are real enough to keep. Mind the light. It is the brightest thing in the house, and the hungriest.

blocks.s: Below, the dark opens into a theatre, and a single figure is taking the stage.

The Ingenue keeps pace with you, and her being here at all, walking, humming, furious and alive after a whole town tried a thousand times to put her in a box, is the only argument you have against everything the mirrors are about to say. "They'll tell you there's only one ending," she says. "Told me that too, in the coffin, every single day. It's the house's favourite lie. There's always another ending. It's just always the harder one to reach." She squeezes your arm. "Whatever it offers you, I'm proof it's lying."

choices.label: Go on toward the thing in the glass
Stay a moment in the hall

blocks.s: You stand your ground, child safe, Understudy broken, and the slow clap comes closer, and with it the pencil, scratching, scratching, the thing that has circled the whole mirror war waiting to see who would be left standing and therefore worth assessing.

It is not ready to be seen yet, that is the next stair, but it can reach, here, for a thing that has just proven its grief is real, and real things are what it takes. You can refuse it the moment, turn, and descend on your own terms. Or you can stand and let it judge the trade you just survived, and find out what the verdict costs.

choices.label: Turn and descend before it finishes deciding
Stand and let it judge you

blocks.s: You walk out the opening door on the strength of your lie, the grief still cold in your fist, the Understudy left grasping at nothing behind you, and you have won, you have beaten the house at its own audition, and the door closes on the mirror floor and you are free with everything you came in carrying.

Except that a person who keeps their grief by lying that they were ready to let it go has become, in the doing, a slightly counterfeit thing, a you with a seam, and as you walk out into the grey you catch your reflection in the last dark pane and it is wearing your coat with the collar turned under wrong, and you cannot, for the life of you, say which of you it was that did the lying. The lie got out. You are not sure you came with it.

You hold with only your body and your refusal, and it is not enough, because the Understudy wears your own strength and knows exactly how you move, and it goes through you the way you would go through yourself, and it takes the child's hand out of your open one, and the absence you have reached for down every stair is, for one breath, filled, and then taken, and then gone.

The Understudy walks off into the glass with the child, wearing your face, carrying your grief, and you are left with an empty open hand and the full understanding that you came all this way, down all these stairs, to lose the thing again, the same way, by being a moment too slow to hold on, which is, a cold voice tells you, exactly how you lost it the first time.

You decide to beat it at its own audition. You arrange your face into surrender, you let your shoulders drop as if the weight is going, you say the words of acceptance and mean none of them, because a thing made to be you can be fooled exactly as far as you can fool yourself, and you have got very good, down all these stairs, at lying to yourself about being ready to let go.
The lie sits cold in you. The Understudy leans in, hungry, believing you, reaching to take what you are only pretending to put down.

choices.label: Spring the trick and seize the upper hand
Feel it see through you

blocks.s: The mirror floor falls away and you come down into a theatre, the realest room in the house, all red plush and gilt and a stage lit so bright the light has weight, and out of the dark of the stalls comes the sound that has followed you since the first night, the slow deliberate clap, and under it the scratch of a pencil. You still have the locket closed in your fist.

lines: THE CURATOR, fond, as of a favourite act:

The brightest room in the house, and the hungriest. Here you will meet our Critic, who decides whether a remembered thing is real enough to keep. Do try to be a little less real than you are. It is, I am afraid, the only protection.

blocks.s: On the stage, something is beginning, slowly, to take a shape with a face.

choices.label: Approach the figure forming on the stage
Find the Caretaker in the box above
Cup your warmest memory close before you go further
Go to the gallery doors at the back of the stalls

blocks.s: The Caretaker is in the box above the stalls, and he does not click his lamp when he sees you, which is somehow worse. "I've stood in this box before," he says. "Many times. I admit the grievers. I light them in. Then I stand up here and I watch what the bright room does to them, and I've never once been let to stop it, and I've never once stopped wanting to."

He looks down at the forming Critic the way a man looks at a thing he has been part of for longer than he can stand. "You should know what it costs, the thing they all come down here chasing. You should know before you reach the bottom. Let me show you the part I've watched too many times."

choices.label: Let him show you the cost
Go down to the gallery yourself

blocks.s: You hold up the locket and its hollow oval, and you do with the memory what the keepsake taught you in the cellar: a grief only weighs what it weighs in the hand of the one who lost the thing it kept. To anyone else the memory is a frame on a wall. To you it is the actual weight of an actual child, and the Critic cannot lift a weight calibrated only to your hands.

The pencil hovers. The composite face considers. And the Critic, which can only take what it can price, cannot price the one thing in the room whose worth is set by love alone, and it turns away, and you keep the name, and you keep yourself.

choices.label: Now reckon with the Heavy's fate

138.title: The Heavy in the Bright Room

blocks.s: You find the Heavy among the frames, and how you find him is how the mirror floor left him. If you held his line, he stands here still a man, counting low, a wall the right way, nodding to you across the lit room. If you used him, what stands here is bigger and flatter, and the Critic has been watching it with frank appetite, because the bright room loves nothing better than a gentle thing turned into a weapon, a memory of kindness lit up exactly so it can be taken.
Either way he looks at you, and either way there is a moment coming, at the bottom of this room, where what he is will pay off, and you both know it.

choices.label: Go on to the stage with him
Stay among the frames a while

blocks.s: You watch the Critic stop before a frame, a memory of a man teaching a child to fish, ordinary, warm, lit. It considers. It makes a note. It decides the afternoon has been remembered hard enough, loved hard enough, to count as real, and being real, it may be taken, and it lifts the frame down, and somewhere in the house a man who has been holding that afternoon a long time feels it go, feels the day he taught his child to fish simply vanish out of him, and does not even get to keep the grief of losing it, because the losing goes too.
That is the mechanism. To be witnessed enough to be real is to be eligible to be taken. The Critic does not destroy memories. It collects them.

choices.label: Look for your own memory among the frames
Go on toward the small stage

blocks.s: You demand to be judged, and you dare the Critic to find you real, because you are done hiding, because after four floors you want, more than safety, to be seen, to have the loss acknowledged by something with the authority to say it counted.

The Critic accepts the dare. It judges. And it finds you real, gloriously, completely real, the realest griever it has assessed in seasons, and being real is being eligible, and it takes you, the whole of you, the grief and the child and the long brave descent, lifts you down off the wall of the world and files you, and somewhere a courteous voice notes that you were a very fine performance, truly, one of the best, and asks, politely, for the house lights, and for the next griever to be admitted to the cold parlour above.

You ask it what it is, and it answers by clapping, slow, and in the rhythm is the answer: it is a piece of the Curator. Not a servant, not a beast the Curator keeps, a fragment of the impresario himself, the hungry attentive part, broken off and given a job. The courteous voice that welcomes you to every floor and the pencil that decides what gets taken are the same thing in two manners.

It is the first real crack in the mystery of what the Curator is, and through it you glimpse something vast and patient and audience-shaped, that runs an endless production not out of cruelty but out of a simple bottomless need to watch, and to keep what it has watched, forever.

choices.label: Take this down into the gallery

blocks.s: You let the Critic have its look. You are tired of holding things deep, tired of paradox, tired of the long defended ache, and you let the bright room see the memory at the centre of you plainly, and the Critic finds it real, as real as anything it has ever assessed, and lifts it down.

The child goes out of you the way the fishing afternoon went out of the man in the gallery: not into grief, into absence, the losing and the lost together. You stand in the bright room no longer sure why you came down all these stairs. Something was here. Something mattered. You reach, out of an old habit your hand no longer understands, for a height beside you that holds nothing, and you cannot think why, and after a while you stop, and the open hand closes, finally, on nothing, forever, and you do not even get to miss what you can no longer remember missing.

You follow the winning soul with your eyes as far as the opening door, out into a wedge of living daylight, and you see them arrive in the world: a person standing in their own kitchen, blinking in ordinary light, whole, content, with a smooth unbothered place in them where a whole human being used to live, and they set down their keys and put the kettle on and feel, all day, every day, that nothing is missing, because the thing that would have told them something was missing was taken along with the missing.

It is the kindest-looking horror you have ever seen. They are not sad. That is the horror. They got everything the house promised and they are not sad and they should be, they should be on the floor, and they never will be again.

choices.label: Consider taking the Encore yourself
Turn toward the naming

blocks.s: You step into the warm borrowed memory, a stranger's kitchen at breakfast, light through a window that was never yours, and you stay, because it is so much easier to stand inside a grief that does not have your specific unbearable shape, and the frame closes around you like a held breath.

You become part of someone else's remembered morning, a figure at the edge of a kitchen that is not yours, in a loss that is not yours, and when the Critic walks the gallery and decides that morning is real enough to take, it lifts the frame down with you inside it, and you are collected along with the toast and the light, filed under a stranger's name, a person who wandered into the wrong grief because their own was one floor too far to carry.

The Critic judges you. It reaches, with its lifted pencil, for the memory at the centre of you, the warm afternoon, the small hand, the whole human being you lost, to decide it is real and lift it down and leave you the smooth emptied peace of the winning soul.

The only defence is paradox: a thing held too deep to be witnessed cannot be judged real enough to take. You can fold the memory down into the part of you the light cannot reach, hidden behind the warmth you carried in, or behind the keepsake that only weighs what it weighs in your hand. Or you can let the Critic have its look, and trust that being seen is survivable, which, in this room, it is not.

choices.label: Hide the memory behind the warmth you kept
Shield it behind the keepsake only you can lift
Let the Critic judge your memory freely
Demand it judge you, and dare it to find you real

blocks.s: You walk the gallery looking for yourself, and you find a wall of frames that are all yours, all the same afternoon from slightly different angles: dust in a bar of light, a chair the right size for someone small, a music box, the bright clutter of a child who was loved. The room at the centre of the whole house, rendered as a hung memory, lit, labelled, waiting for the Critic to decide it is real enough to lift down.

The label under the frames is a name. You make yourself not read it yet. You are not ready. But the wanting to read it, and the not-readiness, both tell you the same thing: these are not memories you witnessed. They are memories you had.

choices.label: Pull your frames down before the Critic reaches them
Leave them hung and lose yourself among the others

blocks.s: On the small stage at the gallery's end, a soul is winning. You watch it happen: a griever who reached the top, who gave the house the grief, being granted the Encore, here, now, in front of you, a demonstration, because the Curator likes you to see the merchandise work.

The door opens. The griever steps toward the living world, lighter, freer, the hole in them closing smooth. And as they go, the bright room lifts down the frames of the one they lost, all of them, every angle of every remembered afternoon, and the griever does not even flinch, because the losing of the grief includes the losing of the lost, and they walk out into life whole and emptied, smiling, missing nothing, mourning no one, free of a weight they will never again know they set down. The Encore is real. The cost is total. You have watched it.

choices.label: Watch them return to the living, emptied
Wonder whether you could take it yourself
Turn from the stage toward the naming

blocks.s: Before you go any deeper into the bright hungry room, you do the thing the Caretaker warned you against on the very first night: you cup your warmest memory close, the small specific warmth of the one you lost, the weight of a smaller hand that fit in yours, and you hold it so deep and so quiet the bright light cannot find an edge of it to take hold of.

It is a risk, carrying something that warm into a room that eats what you love. But a thing held deep enough to be part of you is harder to take than a thing held out where the light can price it, and you would rather risk it than walk in empty.

choices.label: Go to the gallery
Approach the figure on the stage

blocks.s: The Critic, denied your memory, turns its appetite on the nearest real thing, and the nearest real thing is the Heavy, and here is where four books of his counting comes due. If you held his line, he steps in front of the Critic's reach on purpose, a wall the right way one last time, and the only question is whether you let him spend himself for you or stop short to stand with him. If you used him, the brute the bright room has been savouring is already half the Critic's, and it reaches to collect what you made.
He meets your eye. "Go down," he says, the way he said it in the cellar a whole house ago. "The middle's yours. Always was. Let me be the wall, the right way, for once where it counts."

choices.label: Stand with him, then descend to the center carrying the truth
Stop short to mourn him; keep yourself but go no further tonight
Watch the brute you made be collected

blocks.s: It finishes forming, and it is the Critic, and it is made of audience: the held breath, the leaned-forward want, the verdict of every dark room, given a body and a pencil and an appetite. It does not hunt the way the Heavy could be made to hunt. It judges. It decides whether a remembered thing has been witnessed hard enough to count as real, and what is real enough, it is permitted to take.

It regards you with its composite face and makes a small note. You have the sudden cold sense that it is not a separate monster at all. It is the Curator's own attention, sharpened to a point and sent down here to do the part of the work the Curator is too courteous to do himself.

choices.label: Press it on what it really is
Withdraw to the gallery

blocks.s: You will not look at the naming. You have come four floors down and at the threshold of the truth you turn, because some part of you has always turned here, has always gone away from this exact knowing, and you flee back up through the bright room and the gallery and the stalls toward anywhere that is not the centre.

The house lets you. Of course it lets you. It has all the time you do not. You climb back up into the floors you already survived and you wander them, and the name waits at the bottom, unspoken, unlooked-at, and you carry the not-knowing like a stone, and you know, dimly, that this is exactly what you did the first time, and the time before, that the whole season is the record of you coming down to this naming and turning away, and that you will be back, and you will turn away again, until you don't.

152.title: No Voice in Your Ear (an ending)

blocks.s: You refuse the cue. You will not spend a friend to shield yourself, even here, even now, and the Prompter is saved, and goes quiet with something like gratitude, and you walk into the naming with no voice in your ear and the Critic able to hear every true thing you learn.

So when the room gives you the name, and you understand, with your whole body, that the child was yours, the understanding rings out into the bright room loud and clear and real, and the Critic hears it, and lifts its pencil, and there is nothing held deep, nothing folded down, nothing shielded, only a griever who has just learned the truth out loud in the hungriest room in the house. It takes the name. It takes the child. It takes the knowing. You saved your friend, and it was a good thing to do, and it cost you the very thing you came all this way to reach, one breath after you finally reached it.

Behind the stalls is a gallery, long and bright, hung with framed memories the way another gallery hangs paintings: a wedding, a kitchen at breakfast, a hand learning to write, a face caught laughing, each one someone's, each one lit and labelled and waiting. The Critic walks it the way a buyer walks an auction, pausing, considering, making its small notes, and where it decides a memory is real enough, it lifts the frame down, and the memory is gone from whoever had it. Taken. Kept.
The Ingenue is here, if she lived, walking the gallery beside you. The Heavy is here too, somewhere, in whatever state the mirror floor left him.

choices.label: Watch the Critic judge a memory
Wander deeper among the hung memories
Stay close to the Ingenue
Find the Heavy among the frames
Go toward the small stage at the gallery's end

blocks.s: You watch the brute you made be collected. The bright room has wanted this since the cellar, a gentle man turned into a weapon, a memory of kindness lit up precisely so it could be taken, and the Critic lifts him down without effort, because you did the hard part three floors ago when you spoke the word that let the wall be a fist.

And having collected him, the Critic turns, satisfied but not full, never full, to you, the one who made the weapon, and you have no wall now, the right way or the wrong way, between you and the bright hungry attention of a fragment of the Curator. You came down to learn whose grief built the house. You learn it in the last instant, as the pencil descends: yours, and now the house has it, and you, and the gentle man you spent to get here.

The Prompter speaks, and it sounds, at last, like it is dying, because it is. "the naming's next," it says, finishing your dread. "The room will tell you the child's name and that it was yours, and the Critic will be listening, because a name said and known is the realest thing there is and the easiest to take. I can carry the name for you. Hold it so you can know it without saying it, so the Critic can't lift it out of the air. But carrying it spends me. This is the last cue I have. After this there's no voice in your ear, ever again. Take it, and I'm gone, and you walk into the worst room of your life alone but whole. Refuse it, and I'm saved, and you face the naming with the Critic able to hear every true thing you learn."

choices.label: Take the cue; let the Prompter spend itself to shield you
Refuse it; you will not spend a friend to save yourself

blocks.s: The Ingenue takes your arm and walks you through the bright room the way someone walks you through a hospital, steady, talking, keeping you from looking too long at any one lit thing. "Don't read the labels," she says. "Not yet. The room wants you to find your own name before you're ready, because a thing that knows its own name is easier to take. I'll get you to the stage. You need to see what the prize actually costs, with your own eyes, before anyone tells you. Then you can choose instead of being chosen."
She hums, low, the three notes, not from fear now but to give you something steady to walk to.

choices.label: Let her walk you to the stage
Stop and look around the gallery

blocks.s: You fold the memory down into the warmth you cupped at the theatre door, deep, past where the bright light reaches, and you hold the child not out where the room can see and price it but inside, as part of you, where being part of you makes it not a thing you have but a thing you are, and what you are cannot be lifted off a wall.

The Critic's pencil hovers, and finds no edge to take hold of, and after a long terrible consideration it makes a small note and turns away, unsatisfied, and you are intact, and you know the name, and you are still the one who carries it.

choices.label: Now reckon with the Heavy's fate

blocks.s: The door is still open. The offer is still good. You could go now, up and out, and set the grief down and walk into a kitchen of your own with a smooth place where the wound was, and never again reach with an open hand for a height that is not there, and never again feel the specific unbearable shape of the one you lost, because you would not have lost them, because you would never, as far as you would know, have had them.
It is everything you came down here, in some buried way, hoping for. It is also, you now know, the one thing you must not take, because taking it does not heal the loss. It completes it.

choices.label: Reach for the open door and the emptied peace
Turn your back on the door and go toward the naming

blocks.s: You wander deeper, and the memories on the walls stop being labelled with strangers' names and start being warm, start being inviting, start being easier to stand inside than your own, and you feel the pull of it, how restful it would be to step into someone else's remembered kitchen at breakfast and simply stay, to wear a grief that is not yours because it does not have your specific unbearable shape.
The gallery is patient. It does not push. It only hangs its warm lit memories and waits to see whether you would rather live in a stranger's loss than carry your own one floor further.

choices.label: Step into the warm borrowed memory and stay
Wrench yourself back to your own grief and go on

blocks.s: You stand with the Heavy as he holds the Critic's reach one last time, a wall the right way where it counts, and you do not let his sacrifice be for nothing: you go, down, past the bright hungry room, carrying the whole truth intact, the name and the knowing and the warm shielded weight of the child who was yours.

You know it all now. You know the grief is yours. You know the child is the one you lost. You know the Encore would return you to life by lifting the child out of you entire, and that you would walk away smiling and emptied and never know. You know the Critic is a fragment of the Curator, the hungry attentive part, and that the courteous voice and the descending pencil are one thing. And you know there is one floor left, the room at the centre, where the afternoon is kept, and where the choice the whole season has been descending toward is finally yours to make.

lines: THE CURATOR:
You know everything now, or nearly. How rare. How brave. Most of them turn away at the naming. You carried it down.
There is one room left. Your child is in it. Do come. We have kept the seat, and the afternoon, and the choice. We have kept all of it, for you, for so very long.

blocks.s: Below you, the last door in the house stands ajar on a warmth the colour of a kept memory, and the three rising notes are playing, and someone small is waiting to ask if it is real.

You go down the raked aisle toward the stage, and the clapping is coming from the figure now, a thing assembling itself out of applause and attention, a thought becoming a person because enough people looked at it hard enough, and it claps for itself as it forms, an audience and a performer at once.
It has, when it turns, almost a face, and the face is a little bit everyone's, the composite of every audience that ever sat in a dark room and decided whether what it watched was real.

choices.label: Watch it finish forming
Retreat up the aisle to the stalls

gated.aside: It turns its composite face toward you and inclines its head, one judge greeting another. You know this hunger. You have felt the appetite to keep a thing because you found it real. That recognition is the danger.

You see it before it deigns to admit it: this is no separate beast. It is the Curator's own attention, sharpened to a point and sent down to do the work the courtesy upstairs will not. The voice and the pencil are one thing.

blocks.s: You reach the heart of the bright room, and the room gives you, gently, the thing it has held back for four floors: the name under the frames, the name on the empty schoolroom peg, the name in the locket's hollow oval, the name the whole house was built around and has never once been allowed spoken inside.

The child in the high windows, the child with the balloon, the child who asks if it is real, is the one you lost. Not a stranger's child. Not a symbol. Yours. The grief that grew into a house and a town and a hall of mirrors and this bright hungry theatre is your grief, for your child, and you have been walking down through the architecture of your own worst day, coming back, season after season, to a threshold you have never yet been able to cross.
The Critic turns its composite face to you, pencil lifted, to assess whether you, now that you know, are real enough to take.

choices.label: Face the Critic's judgment of your own memory
Refuse to look at the naming; flee the bright room

blocks.s: "The thing at the top of the house," he says, "the door that opens, the way out, the Encore, they call it backstage. The return. The second chance. They sell it as the prize for surviving the production. And it's real. People win it. I've admitted grievers who reached the top and took it and walked back out into a living world." The latch clicks once, slow. "But the Curator lifts a grief the only way he knows. He lifts the thing you grieve. You go back to your life, yes. You go back without the hole. Because you go back without ever having had the one who made it. That's the Encore. You keep your life and you lose the reason you ever wanted it kept."

choices.label: Go down into the gallery, carrying that
Step back into the theatre to steady yourself

164.title: Not Tonight, Not Like This (an ending)

blocks.s: You stop short. You will not let the Heavy spend himself, not even to carry you down, and you step back and stand with him instead, and together you hold at the edge of the bright room, intact, unjudged, the name known and shielded and yours, and you let the descent wait one more night, because you are not willing to reach the centre over the body of a friend.

The Critic, denied, withdraws to the dark of the stalls to sharpen its pencil. The Caretaker comes down from his box, and for once the latch does not click, and he says, almost unbearably gently, "You kept yourself. You kept him. You know the name and you did not pay a friend to learn it. The centre will still be there tomorrow. It always is. Rest, tonight. You've come further than most, and paid less, and that's its own rare brave thing." You have not won. You have not lost. You have refused, one more time, to reach the middle the cheap way, and you are still yourself, and still accompanied, and still, somehow, hoping.

You reach for the open door and the emptied peace, and the house gives it to you gladly, the way it has given every leaving soul exactly what they asked for, and you step up and out into a wedge of living daylight and a kitchen of your own, and the bright room lifts down every frame of the one you lost, all the angles of all the afternoons, and you do not flinch, because the flinch goes too.

You stand in your own kitchen in ordinary light and you put the kettle on and nothing is missing. Nothing will ever be missing. You got everything the house promised. You came down through four floors of your own grief, and at the very moment you understood it was your child, you set the child down for the smooth place where the wound was, and now there is no wound and no child and no you that remembers there was ever either, and the worst of it, the very worst, is that you are content. You are free. You will never know what it cost.

There are no more stairs. The house has spent itself getting you here, and the last of it falls away as you arrive, the halls and the town and the mirrors and the bright room all folding quietly shut behind you, until there is only a short hallway and, at the end of it, a single door with warm light the colour of a kept memory bleeding out around its edges, and the three rising notes playing softly on the other side. You carry the locket, and you carry what the bright room taught you about the cost of leaving.

lines: THE CURATOR, gentle now, almost tender, the showman's polish worn thin:

The last room. We have kept it exactly. The afternoon, the chair, the music, the child. We have kept all of it against your return, season upon season, because you have always, in the end, come back, and turned away, and come back again. This time you have come all the way down. This time you know everything. So this time, perhaps, you will actually choose.

blocks.s: The Caretaker stands before the door, his hand on the latch, not yet clicking it.

choices.label: Go to the Caretaker at the door
Let the child on the other side know you are here
Turn and flee the last door, as you always have

blocks.s: There is movement at the edge of the warm light, and it is the Understudy, the tired part of you, and whatever you did to it on the mirror floor it is here now at the end, reaching, because if there is one thing it has always wanted it is to be the one who decides, to take the choice out of your hands and end the carrying its own way, and its way is to take the child and go and leave you nothing to choose about.

It wears your face, and it reaches with your gesture, and for one cold instant you see the season's worst ending in its open hand: the child taken, the choice stolen, you left at the very centre with empty arms and no decision and no grief and no child, only the smooth nothing of a thing that was decided for you.

choices.label: Get between it and the child; the choice is yours to make
Let it take the choice from you

blocks.s: "Then I open it," he says, "and the choice past it is yours, clean, unblamed, with no door and no Curator and no casting to hold to account. Just you, and the child, and what you decide." And he clicks the latch, once, the small mechanical sound that has come before his every word for five books, and this time it is not a tell. It is the sound of a man who has spent his whole long unpaid sentence letting people into grief, finally getting to let someone choose what to do with it.
He steps aside. "Go on. I've wanted, for forty years, to keep someone out for their own good. Turns out the kinder thing was always to let them in, and then let them decide. Go and decide."

choices.label: Go into the room

blocks.s: You decide to keep the child.

You cannot do it. You reach the very edge of release and you cannot make your open hand close on emptiness one more time, not when the alternative is here, warm, smiling, real enough to hold, and you gather the child to you and you tell the house yes, you will stay, you will take the warm room and the endless afternoon and the child who never has to be lost again, and the house, delighted, closes gently around your choice.

It is not nothing, what you have chosen. It is love, of a kind, the kind that would rather keep the wound open forever than let the beloved recede by a single degree. It is the most understandable choice in the world. It is also a door closing, from the inside, by your own hand.

choices.label: Stay in the warm with the child, forever
At the last second, let go after all

170.title: The Proof a Different Ending Exists

blocks.s: The Ingenue is at the edge of the room, if she lived, and she does not come into the centre, because the centre is yours, but she catches your eye and she hums, low, the three rising notes, the sound of a girl a whole town tried a thousand times to bury who is standing here anyway, alive, furious, proof in the flesh that the house's endings can be refused.

"There's always another ending," she says, one last time. "Even here. Especially here. Whatever they tell you the only two choices are, remember I'm standing here being a third thing nobody scripted. You don't have to take the ending they hand you. You never did."

choices.label: Go to the child
Turn to the choice

171.title: The Choice the House Was Built Around

blocks.s: Here it is. The thing the whole descending house has been shaped to bring you to. The child waits. The afternoon holds. And the choice the Curator has kept for you, season upon season, is finally, simply, yours.

You can give the child up. Let them go, let the afternoon end, let the grief stop being a house and become, at last, a memory that softens and recedes and lets you live. The Curator will call that winning, and offer you the Encore, and you know now exactly what the Encore costs. Or you can keep the child, here, in the warm, where the afternoon never ends, and stay with them forever, and never lose them again because you will never let the losing finish. Or, the Ingenue's voice in your ear, you can refuse the whole wager, decline to be a person who either lets go or holds on by the house's rules at all.

choices.label: Give the child up, and let the grief become a memory
Keep the child, and stay in the warm forever
Refuse the wager itself; neither give up nor stay
Hear the Critic's verdict before you decide
Listen for the Prompter's last cue

gated.aside: Your hands are already open. You spent your grip on the way down, set it down a piece at a time, and there is nothing left in them to pry loose. The hardest thing, without your quite noticing, has already half happened.
You crouch to a height your open hand has always known. Of course you know which choice is the truth. You have been the one waiting at the centre this whole time.

blocks.s: "Then I bar it," he says, and for the first time in forty years the Caretaker gets to be the thing that keeps someone out, and his relief at it is almost unbearable to watch, and he sets himself against the door and will not move. "There. I have kept you out. For your own good. The first time I have ever been allowed."

But barred is not safe, only delayed, and the warm light still bleeds around the edges and the three notes still play, and you will have to find another way in, a harder way, a crack or a window or the sheer refusal to be kept from your own child, or you will have to give up at the very threshold and let the not-knowing win.

choices.label: Find the harder way in
Give up at the barred door and turn back

blocks.s: You let the curtain fall, understanding fully what you have done, and the house lets its breath out, all of it, every floor you descended releasing its held breath at once, the cold parlour and the buried town and the hall of mirrors and the bright hungry theatre all softening, all finally allowed to become memory instead of architecture, because you let the afternoon at their centre end.

The child is gone, up and out, the way you let them go. The grief remains, but it is grief now, soft and grey and survivable, a thing you carry instead of a thing you live inside. And the Critic, at the edge of the warm light, does the thing that costs it everything: it brings its hands together, slowly, and claps, honestly, approving a release, admitting out loud that a thing can be real and be let go. The admission cracks the only law it ever had. It is the most expensive applause in the house, and it is for you.

lines: THE CURATOR:
You let go. After so long, after so many returns, you finally let go.

You have won the Haunt, and the Encore is yours, and you know, now, what it costs, which is the only honest way it has ever been offered. Take it, or do not. You have already done the thing that mattered. The rest is only a door.
The Company has played its parts. The grief is laid down. The house is quiet.

But the dead do not retire, dear one. We only recast. Rest, all of you. There is another production, and another grief that grew too large, and another stage going dark, and we will know each other by our tells when the lights come up.
Thank you. You were, all of you, magnificent. Curtain.

blocks.s: Your hand is open at your side, and for the first time in five books it is not reaching. It is simply open. Somewhere, far off, the lights come up on a different stage, and a Company that has just laid one grief down turns, recognisable by the tells they cannot help, toward the next.

You begin to refuse the wager, you reach for the third door, the one the Curator did not build, and at the decisive moment you falter, because making a door no one built is harder than walking through one of the two on offer, and in the faltering the house, which had shuddered, steadies, and closes on your half-made defiance.

The Curator's certainty returns like a tide. "A fine attempt," he says, and means it, the way an impresario means it about a performer who reached past their range. "They so rarely even try. But the third door has to be finished, you see, not merely begun, and you have only ever been allowed to begin." And the warm room takes you, defiance and all, and adds your beautiful unfinished refusal to the long catalogue of beautiful unfinished things the house has kept, and the show goes on, because the show always goes on, for anyone who reaches but does not quite arrive.

You refuse the frame entirely. You are not a contestant to be admitted or a danger to be barred. You are a person who lost a child, and you will not let the house arrange you into a role at the very threshold of the truth, not even a kind one, not even his.

The Caretaker looks at you, and something lifts in his tired face, something like hope he has not let himself feel in a long time. "Oh," he says softly. "You're going to make them earn it. Good. Make them all earn it. There's one more who'll try to tell you what this is."

choices.label: Go in
Face the one who tries to frame you next

blocks.s: You set the locket on the small shelf, in the empty place shaped for it, and it fits, and for the first time since the cellar the keepsake is not a weight in your fist but an object at rest in the room it came from, and the hollow oval inside it does not feel like absence any more but like a frame waiting, patiently, to hold a face you are finally allowed to remember instead of forced to guard.
Something in the room eases. Not the grief. The grip. The locket is home. Whatever you choose now, you have done one true thing. You have let one piece of the afternoon be still.

choices.label: Turn to the choice

blocks.s: You decide to let the child go.

You take the small hand in your open one, the absence finally filled so you can feel, exactly, what you are about to release, and you tell the child the truth the whole house was built to avoid: that it was real, all of it, the afternoon and the love and the small specific life, real and yours and not unmade by ending; that letting them go is not pretending they never were but finally allowing that they were, and are, and are over; and that you cannot keep an afternoon sharp forever without keeping the wound open under it forever too.

The child smiles, the way they did in the very first window, and does not ask if it is real again, because you have just answered it. The grip eases. The house, all around and below and above, begins, very slowly, to let its breath out.

choices.label: Let the curtain fall, understanding fully what you have done
Say goodbye to the child you let yourself be seen by
Release them, but without fully grasping the cost

blocks.s: You put your hand flat against the warm door and you let the child on the other side know you are here, fully, openly, the way you have not let yourself be seen since the first window, and through the wood a small hand presses back against yours, palm to palm, and a voice you would know anywhere says, muffled and certain, "You came all the way down. You actually came all the way down this time."

The absence in your open hand is, through the door, for the length of one held breath, filled. You let it be seen that it is filled. Whatever you do past this door, you will do it having admitted, out loud, to the child and to yourself, exactly what you came here holding.

choices.label: Go to the Caretaker at the door
Open the door and go in

gated.aside: Through the door, the small hand finds yours and holds, palm to palm, and the Curator, who has a remark for everything, for once says nothing at all.

blocks.s: You release the child, and it is the right thing, and you do it, but you do it without fully grasping what the bright room tried to teach you, and so the letting-go is a tearing rather than a leave-taking, a wound reopened rather than a wound allowed to close.

The child goes. The afternoon ends. But because you did not let yourself understand it fully, did not let yourself be seen, did not set the keepsake down or carry the cost with open eyes, the release does not free you the way it could have. You walk out of the emptied room having done the brave thing clumsily, half of you still clenched around the loss you just released, and the Curator offers you the Encore in a voice that is kind, and you know, dimly, too late, that you are about to accept a mercy you never fully chose. It is not the worst ending. It is only the one where the right thing was done without the grace that would have made it whole.

180.title: Kept Out, For Your Own Good (an ending)

blocks.s: You let him bar it, and you do not look for the harder way, and you let the Caretaker keep you out, for your own good, the first and only time he has ever been allowed to, and the two of you stand in the short hallway with the warm light bleeding around the edges of a door that will not open because you have asked it not to.

It is, in its way, a mercy. You do not have to choose. You do not have to let go or stay. You only have to stand here, kept safe from your own grief by a kind tired man, forever, a single door away from the child, never crossing, never deciding, never losing them and never holding them, and the Caretaker stands with you and does not click his latch, and you both pretend, as the long grey not-night stretches out with no morning in it, that being kept from the choice is the same as being spared it.

You go in, however you came to it, and the room is the one you saw on the first night through a door you could not make yourself look past: small and warm and full of a single afternoon, dust standing in a bar of light, a chair the right size for someone small, a music box with its lid up playing the three rising notes, and on the floor the bright clutter of a child who was loved.

And the child is here. Not glimpsed in a window, not behind glass, not asking through a door. Here, in the warm light, looking up at you, balloon string wound twice around one small fist. The grief that built the whole house is standing in front of you wearing the face you have spent five books and however many seasons refusing to look at, and it is, the bright room told you and your own heart confirms, yours.

choices.label: Go to the child
Look first at the kept afternoon
Find the Ingenue at the room's edge

blocks.s: You go to the child and you crouch to their height, the height your open hand has been finding all this time, and the child looks at you with the question that has followed you down every floor, except now it is not muffled by a window or a door. It is just here, between you, in the warm light.

"Is it real?" the child asks. "Was it real? If you let me go, was any of it real, or does letting me go mean it never happened?" And then, smaller, the thing the whole house was built to keep you from having to answer: "And if you keep me here, where it's warm, where the afternoon never ends, is that me? Or is that just the room?"

choices.label: Turn to the choice the room has kept for you
Something wearing your tired face reaches for the child

blocks.s: You finish it. You refuse the wager all the way down, neither performing the release nor staying to feed the show, and you take the refusal you hardened at the threshold and make of it a door the Curator did not build, and it costs you, more than either of his would have, because an unscripted thing has no kept seat and no soft landing and no applause.

But you walk through it, with the child's hand in yours, on your own terms, out of the centre of the house and into something the Curator does not have a name for, because you have done the one thing an endless production cannot absorb: you have left without losing and stayed without surrendering, refused to be either of the endings on offer. And somewhere a courteous voice that is, for the first time in the whole long run of the show, genuinely uncertain, says, very quietly, to no one, "...well. That is new." And new, in a house that has run the same two endings forever, is the most dangerous and the most hopeful thing there is. The Company will remember that it can be done. So will you. So, you suspect, will the Curator, which is exactly the problem you have just become.
You stay.

You keep the child, and the warm room closes gently around the two of you, and the afternoon does not end, ever, the dust hanging in its bar of light, the music box playing its three notes, the small chair and the bright clutter and the child, here, warm, real, never to be lost because you will never let the losing finish. And it is, for a long time, everything you wanted. You have your child back. The wound is not healed but it is held open at exactly the temperature of love, and you would not have it any other way, and you tell yourself that, and on most of the endless afternoons you believe it.

You will never leave. That is the price, and you paid it gladly, and it is the most understandable thing a grieving person ever did. Far above, the house keeps you both, a parent and a child in a kept afternoon, and the Curator, who got to keep you after all, lowers the lights on the warm room with something that is almost, but not quite, respect, because staying took a kind of love too, even if it was the kind that builds a house instead of leaving one.

You look at the afternoon the house has kept, and it is unbearable in its ordinariness, one good day held perfect against decay, the kind of day you did not know to treasure while it was happening because you thought there would be a thousand more. The house has kept this one, exactly, the dust and the light and the small chair, because the one who built the house could not let it become a memory, could not let it soften and yellow and recede, and so kept it sharp and present and eternal, which is another word for never letting it end, which is another word for never letting the grief move.
The locket is warm in your hand. There is a place in this afternoon where it belongs, on a small shelf where the child kept their treasures, an empty place shaped exactly for it.

choices.label: Set the locket where it belongs in the afternoon
Turn to the choice

186.title: The Last Cue, or the Silence

blocks.s: You listen for the voice that has finished your thoughts since the first vent. If it spent itself in the bright room to shield you, there is only silence now, a clean profound silence where the cue would be, and the silence is itself the last cue: this thought, this one, you finish alone, because some things a person has to say in their own voice with no one a half-beat ahead.

And if it lived, it speaks once, soft, and for the first time it does not finish your thought early. It waits for you to have it. "Go on," it says, after you, behind you, letting you lead. "Whatever you choose. I've got no cue for this one. This one was always going to be yours."

choices.label: Make the choice

blocks.s: You refuse the wager itself. You will not give the child up to earn a prize and you will not keep them to lose yourself; you reject the whole frame in which a parent's grief is a transaction with two doors, the way you refused the Caretaker's roles and the Critic's verdict, and you turn the refusal you hardened at the threshold on the Curator himself.

You say: no. Not your wager. Not your two endings. I'll carry this my own way, neither performing the release for your applause nor staying to feed your endless show, and I will find, or make, a third door you did not build, even if it costs more than either of yours. The warm room shudders. The Curator's courteous voice, for the first time in five books, is not sure what comes next, and a not-sure Curator is the most dangerous and the most hopeful thing you have yet made happen.

choices.label: Pay the price of refusing the Curator to his face
Falter, and let the house close on your defiance

blocks.s: You let it take the choice from you. The Understudy, the tired part of you, reaches past your hesitation and gathers up the child and your gesture and your decision all at once, and walks out of the warm light wearing your face, and the choice is made, not by you, the easy way, the way that asks nothing, and you are left at the centre of the whole house with empty arms.

And it is exactly as terrible as it was always going to be: no grief, because the grief went with the child, and no child, and no choice, and no you that did the choosing, only the smooth emptied nothing of a person who came all the way down to the one decision that was theirs to make and let the tired part of themselves make it instead. The afternoon goes dark. The music box winds down. Somewhere a courteous voice sighs, and files the season under so very nearly, and prints a fresh card.

You turn from the last door, as you have turned from it season upon season, because the warm light and the three notes and the small hand pressing from the other side are more than you can make yourself face, and the house, patient as ever, lets you go.

You climb back up through floors that rebuild themselves around you, the bright room and the mirrors and the town and the cold parlour, and somewhere along the way the knowing thins and softens and is gone, and you wake on a chaise the colour of old wine, in a parlour cold enough to show your breath, and you do not remember lying down, and there is a card propped against a dead candle, and far off and above, someone is humming three rising notes. You have been here before. You will be here again. You always come back, and you always turn away, and the only thing that ever changes is that the Curator keeps your seat.

Because you let yourself be seen at the door, because you admitted out loud and palm to palm exactly what you carried, the goodbye is a real one, not a tearing-away but a leave-taking, and the child gets to say it back, gets to be a person saying goodbye and not just a grief being set down.

"You came all the way down," the child says, one last time, and winds the balloon string off their fist and lets it go, and the balloon drifts up into the warm light and does not come back, and the child watches it go without fear. "That's how I'll know it was real," they say. "You came all the way down, and then you let me go up." And the afternoon, at last, is allowed to end.

choices.label: Let the curtain fall

blocks.s: "Here's where my part finally means something," the Caretaker says, and his voice shakes, because this is the moment four books have walked him toward. "Forty years of doors, and they all come down to this one. I'm the one who lets people in. I've let you in, to all of it, every season, knowing what was at the bottom, and never once been let to keep you out for your own good." His thumb rests on the latch. "So tonight I'm going to do the one thing my casting forbids. I'm going to ask you. Not admit you, not bar you. Ask. Do you want me to open this door and let you carry what's past it yourself? Or do you want me to bar it, and make you find a harder way, and have something to blame that isn't your own choice?"

choices.label: Ask him to open it; you will carry the choice yourself
Refuse to be admitted OR barred; you are not a contestant
Ask him to bar it, for your own good

gated.aside: He looks at you, and something in his tired face eases, as if for once he is not the only one in the house who has spent a life working other people's doors.

choice.label: You have kept doors too. Take the latch from his hand and open this one yourself.
You did not come all this way to be admitted or barred. Open it yourself.

blocks.s: It is there at the door's edge, the Critic, the Curator's hungry attentive fragment, pencil lifted, ready to tell you what your grief is worth, whether it is real enough, what verdict applies. It opens its composite mouth to judge.

And you refuse it. You tell it, plainly, that your loss is not a performance to be assessed and your child is not a memory to be priced, and that you will not accept a verdict on the realest thing you have ever carried, not from a fragment of a thing that runs an endless show out of a bottomless need to watch. The pencil hesitates. You have done a thing the house has no script for. You have declined to be judged at all, and something in you hardens into a refusal bigger than this room, bigger than this season.

choices.label: Go into the room, unjudged

blocks.s: The Critic is here at the centre, and at the choice it does the thing that is its own arc's payoff: it must decide whether to grant or withhold its applause for what you do, and granting it costs the Critic, because to applaud a release honestly is to admit that a thing can be real and also be let go, that being witnessed is not the same as being kept, and that admission cracks the only law it has ever known.

It lifts its hands. It does not yet clap. It is waiting, you realise, to see whether you will do something worth the cost of approving, something that would make a fragment of the Curator willing to spend itself to say: yes, that was real, and you were right to let it go.

choices.label: Give the child up
Keep the child
